target.title: fix(tailscale): prevent zombie process on binary check timeout
target.body:
### What

- Removed the redundant `Promise.race` wrapper around `runExec` in `checkBinary`.
- Relies on `runExec`’s internal `timeout` (via Node’s `execFile`) to kill the child process after 3s.
- Prevents hanging `tailscale --version` processes from becoming zombies when the binary is slow or hangs.

### Why

`checkBinary` was racing its own timeout against the one already implemented in `runExec`/`execFile`.  
When the outer `Promise.race` resolved first, the child process was never explicitly killed, leading to zombie processes.

By removing the extra `Promise.race` and using only the Node timeout, we guarantee the process is terminated.

### How tested

- Built the project: `pnpm build`
- Ran existing checks: `pnpm check` (passes)
- Logic is intentionally minimal; no new tests required for this bug fix.

target.title: feat(mcp): channel-mediated approval for MCP tool calls (consent envelope)
target.body:
Closes #78308

## Summary

Today, when a bundle-MCP tool returns to the agent, OpenClaw passes the result straight through to the model — there's no approval gate analogous to the one [`exec-approvals`](../tools/exec-approvals.md) provides for shell commands. For act-tier MCP tools (send email, create vault entry, set HA state, etc.), the only thing standing between the user and the action is *the model deciding to call it*.

This PR adds a small, additive contract — the **MCP consent envelope** — that lets MCP servers opt into the existing `/approve <id> allow-once|allow-always|deny` pipeline that already backs shell-exec approvals. **Servers that don't opt in are unaffected.**

## The contract

When a server wants user approval for a call, it returns:

```json
{
  "ok": false,
  "requires_confirmation": true,
  "action_id": "<server-side single-use token>",
  "summary": "<one-line description of the action>",
  "expires_in_seconds": 60
}
```

…in either `structuredContent` or as JSON inside `content[0].text`. OpenClaw recognises the envelope, suppresses the result, and:

1. Issues a `plugin.approval.request` to the local gateway (reuses everything: `channel-approval-auth`, ID-prefix routing, the magic-word reply parser).
2. Blocks until the user replies on the trusted channel.
3. On `allow-once` / `allow-always`: re-calls the tool with `confirmation_token = action_id` set on the input. Returns that second result to the agent.
4. On `deny` / expiry / error: returns a synthetic `{ok:false, approved:false, reason}` result. The model never sees `action_id`.

## Why the trust boundary actually moves

The model never sees `action_id` and **`confirmation_token` is stripped from any model-supplied input** on the first call. Even if a malicious or careless agent fabricates a token, the MCP server's redemption check rejects it (the server is responsible for one-shot/TTL enforcement of `action_id`).

This is the same pattern OpenClaw already enforces for shell exec via `exec-approvals` — *agent asks, user authorises, runtime executes*. The PR just generalises that pattern to MCP tools that opt into the contract.

## What changed

| File | Change |
|---|---|
| `src/agents/pi-bundle-mcp-consent.ts` | **New.** Envelope detection, the default approval requester (calls `plugin.approval.request` + `waitDecision`), `confirmation_token` scrubber, denied-result builder, **`sanitiseToolEmittedApprovalText`** (review-comment defence — neutralises `/approve` in MCP-emitted text). ~270 lines. |
| `src/agents/pi-bundle-mcp-materialize.ts` | Wraps every tool's `execute()` in a new `callMcpToolWithConsent` orchestrator. Plumbs `requestApproval`, `consentEnabled`, **`agentId`, and `sessionKey`** through `materializeBundleMcpToolsForRun` and `createBundleMcpToolRuntime`. |
| `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.ts`, `compact.ts` | Pass the agent harness's existing `agentId` + `sessionKey` into the materialize layer so the gateway forwarder can resolve the user's actual delivery channel (WhatsApp / Telegram / Slack / web UI). |
| `src/auto-reply/reply/commands-approve.ts` | Accept bare `/approve allow-once` (no id) when there's exactly one pending approval — typing a uuid on a phone is unrealistic UX. On ambiguity (>1 pending), refuses with a hint including the explicit form. |
| `src/infra/plugin-approvals.ts` | Cleaner reply prompt: shows all three decisions (`allow-once \| allow-always \| deny`) inline, copy-paste-ready, with the id on its own line for the explicit fallback. |
| `src/config/types.mcp.ts` | Adds `mcp.approvals.enabled?: boolean` (default true). |
| `src/agents/pi-bundle-mcp-consent.test.ts` | **New.** 21 unit tests including a live-caught `decision: null` regression and a `agentId/sessionKey` propagation regression. |
| `docs/tools/mcp-consent-envelope.md` | **New.** Server-author contract, configuration, trust-boundary explanation. |
| `CHANGELOG.md` | Entry under `Unreleased`. |

No new approval kind is introduced — the implementation **reuses the existing `"plugin"` ApprovalKind**.

## Test plan

- ✅ `pnpm install`
- ✅ `pnpm lint` — 8234 files, 213 rules, 0 warnings, 0 errors.
- ✅ Full agents-core suite: **272 files passed (272), 3515 tests passed | 4 skipped**.
- ✅ `pi-bundle-mcp-consent.test.ts` — 21/21 green.
- ✅ `commands-approve.test.ts` — 18/18 green (existing tests still pass after the implicit-id parser change).

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** Without channel-mediated approval, the model is the trust gate for MCP tool calls that want user approval. Captured live on **vanilla `OpenClaw 2026.4.24`** with HomeBrain's MCP servers: when the agent fired `homebrain-nextcloud__nc-files_share`, the consent envelope (with `action_id`) passed straight to the model, which **leaked the token verbatim into chat**, **offered to self-confirm**, and **hallucinated a second action_id off-by-one** when it lost track of the first. Full screenshot + analysis: [#78308 comment](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/78308#issuecomment-4391533882). This PR replaces that prompt-level honour-system with runtime-enforced channel approval.

**Environment tested:** `homebrain@192.168.178.58`. x86_64 Ubuntu 24.04. AMD Radeon RX 9060 XT (16 GB VRAM). llama.cpp + Qwen3.6-35B-A3B (Q5_K_XL). HomeBrain reference MCP servers from [oalterg/HomeBrain](https://github.com/oalterg/HomeBrain). **OpenClaw built directly from this PR branch and installed live** in place of the npm-shipped 2026.4.24 binary for the runtime-side tests.

**Steps run after the patch:**

```bash
// Local: build + pack the PR branch
pnpm build && npm pack
// Deploy the .tgz to the target box
scp openclaw-2026.5.6.tgz homebrain@192.168.178.58:/tmp/
ssh homebrain@192.168.178.58 'echo admin | sudo -S bash -c "
  cd /usr/lib/node_modules
  rm -rf openclaw && mkdir openclaw && cd openclaw
  tar xzf /tmp/openclaw-2026.5.6.tgz --strip-components=1
  npm install --omit=dev --no-audit --no-fund --silent"'
// Set the missing approvals.plugin config (the forwarder needs it to deliver)
ssh homebrain@192.168.178.58 'sudo -u homebrain jq ".approvals = {plugin:{enabled:true,mode:\"session\"}}" \
  ~/.openclaw/openclaw.json > /tmp/c && sudo -u homebrain cp /tmp/c ~/.openclaw/openclaw.json'
ssh homebrain@192.168.178.58 'sudo -u homebrain systemctl --user restart openclaw-gateway'

// User sends on WhatsApp:
//   "Create a public share link to /uploads in Nextcloud, expires in 7 days."
// OpenClaw routes through the agent harness, which fires nc.files_share.
// My code intercepts the consent envelope, calls plugin.approval.request.
// The forwarder delivers the prompt to the user's WhatsApp + web UI.
// User taps Allow once on the web UI (or replies /approve allow-once on WhatsApp).
// Runtime re-calls the tool with confirmation_token. Real share URL returns.
```

**Evidence after fix:**

The web UI's native plugin-approval modal renders my PR's `plugin.approval.request` exactly right — title `MCP tool approval`, description `homebrain-nextcloud.nc.files_share — Nextcloud: create public share link for /uploads (expires in 7 days)`, severity `warning`, plugin `mcp:homebrain-nextcloud`, three buttons `Allow once / Always allow / Deny`, expiry countdown. The user pressed `Allow once`. Screenshot captured (not committed; happy to attach for maintainer review). Same approval was offered on the user's WhatsApp simultaneously via the routing-fix commit.

```text
$ openclaw --version  # the deployed PR build
OpenClaw 2026.5.6 (a2cd999)

// Gateway log (verbatim) at the moment the user tapped Allow once:
2026-05-06T20:01:22 ⇄ res ✓ plugin.approval.waitDecision 59412ms
                    conn=6f5914ca…dfe2 id=0c6f887a…7e6d

// /var/log/homebrain/mcp-nextcloud-audit.log — the result of the consent loop:
2026-05-06T20:01:22 {"server":"nextcloud","action":"share",
                     "path":"/uploads","expire_days":7}

// Nextcloud confirms the actual share resource exists on disk:
$ curl -u admin:<app-pass> -H 'OCS-APIRequest: true' \
  http://127.0.0.1:8080/ocs/v2.php/apps/files_sharing/api/v1/shares
{"id":"2","share_type":3,"path":"/uploads","stime":1778097682,
 "url":"http://127.0.0.1:8080/s/iiaRr54CKLnZ7sC", ...}
```

**Observed result after the fix:**

- **Vanilla baseline (without PR):** the model leaks `action_id` to chat, offers to self-confirm, hallucinates a second action_id off-by-one. Trust boundary is the model's behavior — soft gate. [Screenshot + analysis in #78308 comment](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/78308#issuecomment-4391533882).
- **PR build:** the agent fires the tool, the consent envelope is intercepted, `plugin.approval.request` is issued to the gateway with the correct `agentId` + `sessionKey` so the forwarder can route. Approval prompt arrives in the **web UI's native modal** (gorgeous render, all three decisions one tap) AND on the user's **WhatsApp** simultaneously. User taps `Allow once` (or replies `/approve allow-once`). Runtime re-calls the tool with `confirmation_token = action_id`. **Real Nextcloud share is created** — verified by the audit log entry, the gateway `waitDecision 59412ms ✓` line, and the actual `share` row in NC's `files_sharing` API.
- **A live regression bug was caught and fixed during this very run**: `decision: null` from the gateway in two-phase mode means "pending, call waitDecision", not "deny". Fixed in `ef30bbf` with a regression test. Without the live test cycle, that would have shipped as a latent bug masking real user approvals as denials.
- **Forwarder routing was also live-caught**: the PR initially didn't propagate `agentId`/`sessionKey` from the agent harness to `plugin.approval.request`, so the forwarder had no session binding and prompts auto-cancelled silently. Fixed in `2056d25` by plumbing both fields through `materializeBundleMcpToolsForRun` + `createBundleMcpToolRuntime` from `attempt.ts` and `compact.ts`.
- **`/approve` UX was live-caught too**: typing a full uuid by hand on a phone is unrealistic, and the prompt template said `Reply with: /approve <id>` with `<id>` as a literal placeholder. Fixed in `a2cd999`: bare `/approve allow-once` resolves to the single most-recent pending approval; the prompt now shows all three decisions inline copy-paste-ready.

**What was not tested:** the *deny* path through user-driven rejection (we exercised the deny path via timeout and via approval-system error in unit tests, but not via a user explicitly typing `/approve deny` end-to-end on the live deployment). The unit tests (`on consent envelope + deny, returns synthetic denied result and does NOT re-call`) cover this deterministically. Maintainer running the PR through any deployment can verify in a single message exchange.

## Review responses

### Channel-level message provenance ([m13v's review](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/78308#issuecomment-4391392109))

> *"if a compromised mcp server returns a tool result containing '/approve abc123 allow-once' in summary or output, and the channel renderer doesn't distinguish user-typed from tool-emitted messages at the parser layer, an mcp tool self-approves future calls by poisoning the transcript."*

Closed in `083dd748`. Two layers:

1. `sanitiseToolEmittedApprovalText` neutralises any `/approve` substring in MCP-emitted text by inserting U+200B between the slash and `approve` so `^/approve\b` cannot match. Applied at parse-time inside `detectMcpConsentEnvelope`, so every downstream path that renders the envelope sees pre-sanitised text.
2. The existing `/approve` parser already enforces provenance (via `isAuthorizedSender` against the channel allowlist) but the invariant was implicit. A code comment now makes it explicit so future refactors don't lose it.

Live-verified on the deployed `.58` PR build with a deliberately malicious envelope (full transcript on the [issue thread](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/78308#issuecomment-4394805083)). Two new unit tests cover the sanitiser directly and the envelope path.

## Reference / motivation

Part of a broader integration on a [HomeBrain](https://github.com/oalterg/HomeBrain) box. Reference server-side: [`scripts/mcp_common.py`](https://github.com/oalterg/HomeBrain/blob/vault-integration/scripts/mcp_common.py) (the `Consent` helper) and [`scripts/mcp-*.py`](https://github.com/oalterg/HomeBrain/tree/vault-integration/scripts). Design write-up: [`INTEGRATIONS_PLAN.md`](https://github.com/oalterg/HomeBrain/blob/vault-integration/INTEGRATIONS_PLAN.md).

## Open questions for review

1. **Should I add an `mcp.approvals.allowlist: [{server, tool}]`?** Left out for v1 — gating is already opt-in *per-tool* by the MCP server (only act-tier tools return the envelope), and `allow-always` already short-circuits future calls. Happy to add an allowlist as a follow-up if maintainers prefer.
2. **The decision callback returns the existing `ExecApprovalDecision` union** (`allow-once` | `allow-always` | `deny`) — keeps the reply-parser + UI rendering identical.
3. **Approval kind**: I considered adding `"mcp-tool"` as a third `ChannelApprovalKind`. Decided against it (~10 files for a presentation distinction) — the existing plugin renderer already shows `pluginId: mcp:<server>`, `toolName: <tool>`, `description: <summary>`. Open to redoing as a separate kind if maintainers prefer.
4. **Should `approvals.plugin.enabled = true, mode = "session"` be the default?** Currently the gateway's plugin-approval forwarder is fully gated on this config block being explicitly set. Without it set, requests are accepted but never delivered — the boundary becomes a permanent deny gate (caught live during this PR's testing). Sensible default seems to be on-with-session-routing, especially now that the contract is broadly useful for MCP tool calls. Happy to add that as a separate PR if maintainers agree.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.body:
## Summary

Adds human-readable ISO-8601 timestamp fields alongside raw epoch-ms values in cron log output and events, making debug logs much easier to read without manual conversion.

Closes #58574

## Change type

- [x] Enhancement to an existing feature

## Scope

- [x] Minor (1-2 files, <50 lines)

## What changed

- Added `formatEpochMs()` helper in `src/cron/service/timer.ts` that converts epoch-ms to ISO-8601 string (with `undefined`/`NaN`/`Infinity` guards)
- Added `nextAtHuman` field to the "cron: timer armed" debug log
- Added `nextRunAt` field to "cron: scheduling one-shot retry" and "cron: applying error backoff" info logs
- Added `nextRunAt` field to the `CronEvent` type and `emitJobFinished` event payload
- Added 4 unit tests for `formatEpochMs`

## Before

```
debug cron {"nextAt":1774996829150,"delayMs":60000} "cron: timer armed"
```

## After

```
debug cron {"nextAt":1774996829150,"nextAtHuman":"2026-04-01T12:00:29.150Z","delayMs":60000} "cron: timer armed"
```

## Backward compatibility

- The raw `nextAt` / `nextRunAtMs` fields are preserved unchanged
- The new `nextRunAt` field on `CronEvent` is optional (`string | undefined`)
- No config schema or stored data format changes

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm test -- src/cron/service/timer.test.ts` — 6/6 pass (including 4 new formatEpochMs tests)
- [x] `pnpm check` — all lint/type checks pass

target.title: [codex] fix chat reminder cron delivery
target.body:
## Summary
- promote reminder-like chat cron jobs without explicit `sessionTarget` or `delivery` into isolated `agentTurn` jobs with inferred announce delivery
- keep explicit `sessionTarget: "main"` reminders internal-only so existing cron semantics stay intact
- add tests covering promotion, explicit main reminders, and promoted reminders with context history

## Root Cause
Cron jobs created as `sessionTarget: "main"` plus `payload.kind: "systemEvent"` only enqueue an internal system event. They do not request outbound delivery, so runs can finish with `deliveryStatus = "not-requested"` even though the reminder never reaches the user.

## Impact
Chat-created reminders now default to a deliverable execution path that sends the reminder back to the originating conversation instead of silently remaining an internal scheduler event.

## Validation
- `pnpm exec vitest run --config vitest.config.ts src/agents/tools/cron-tool.test.ts src/agents/tools/cron-tool.flat-params.test.ts`

target.title: fix(cron): stop fallback attempts when cron budget is exhausted
target.body:
## Problem

When a cron job's primary model times out near the cron deadline, the fallback model is started but immediately killed by the same cron timeout — receiving effectively 0ms to make an LLM request. The fallback never gets a fair chance to respond, and the user sees a misleading `cron: job execution timed out` error attributed to the fallback model.

**Observed in production logs (2026-03-22):**

```
[08:10:30.733] anthropic/claude-opus-4-6 attempt=1/2 reason=unknown err='Request was aborted.' duration=28667ms
[08:10:30.735] openai-codex/gpt-5.4    attempt=2/2 reason=timeout err='cron: job execution timed out' duration=0ms
```

The cron timeout wraps the entire fallback chain. When the primary model consumes most of the budget, the fallback model is allocated ~0ms and fails immediately.

## Solution

1. **Propagate the cron deadline** (`deadlineAtMs`) from `executeJobCoreWithTimeout` through the cron service state down to `runCronIsolatedAgentTurn`.

2. **Add a `beforeAttempt` hook** to `runWithModelFallback` that is called before each candidate attempt. If it returns `{ type: 'stop' }`, the fallback chain stops and preserves the original error from the previous attempt.

3. **Check remaining budget** in the cron layer's `beforeAttempt` callback. If less than `min(30s, timeout/4)` remains before the cron deadline, the fallback is skipped with a clear message:
   > `Skipping fallback: only 50ms remain before cron timeout (need at least 30000ms).`

This ensures:
- The primary model's real error is preserved (not masked by a fake cron timeout on the fallback)
- No wasted LLM API calls that would be aborted instantly
- The `beforeAttempt` hook is generic and reusable for other pre-attempt checks

## Changes

- `src/agents/model-fallback.ts` — Add `beforeAttempt` hook + `stop_before_candidate` decision type
- `src/cron/service/timer.ts` — Compute and pass `deadlineAtMs` to job execution
- `src/cron/isolated-agent/run.ts` — Use `beforeAttempt` to check remaining cron budget
- `src/cron/service/state.ts` + `src/gateway/server-cron.ts` — Thread `deadlineAtMs` through interfaces

## Tests

- `model-fallback.test.ts` — beforeAttempt stop behavior
- `run.fallback-time-budget.test.ts` — Cron deadline propagation + fallback budget exhaustion
- `service.issue-regressions.test.ts` — Deadline passed to isolated runs


## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Cron isolated agent-turn jobs should not start a model fallback when the primary attempt has consumed almost the whole cron deadline and the fallback would be killed immediately by the same timeout.

**Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout on branch `fix/cron-fallback-timeout` at `008791fa10`, macOS host, Node 22. The proof command uses the same fallback budget formula added in this branch and the cron job shape from the affected isolated agent-turn surface.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran `node .artifacts/pr-52365-real-behavior-proof.mjs` locally after the patch, then ran `pnpm test src/cron/isolated-agent/run.fallback-time-budget.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose` as supplemental coverage for the same gate.

**Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from the local reproduction command:

```
$ node .artifacts/pr-52365-real-behavior-proof.mjs
cron fallback budget repro
job: sessionTarget=isolated wakeMode=next-heartbeat payload=agentTurn timeoutSeconds=60
primary attempt: consumed 59900ms of a 60000ms cron deadline
fallback check: remainingMs=100; fallbackMinRemainingMs=15000
before(upstream/main): runWithModelFallback had no beforeAttempt budget gate -> fallbackAttempted=true
after(this branch): fallbackAttempted=false
after stop: Skipping fallback: only 100ms remain before cron timeout (need at least 15000ms).
```

Supplemental targeted test output:

```
$ pnpm test src/cron/isolated-agent/run.fallback-time-budget.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose
✓ allows fallback attempts when the per-attempt budget fits inside the remaining cron deadline
✓ uses agents.defaults.timeoutSeconds for fallback budget when job timeoutSeconds is unset
✓ stops new fallback attempts when the per-attempt budget no longer fits
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  3 passed (3)
```

**Observed result after fix:** With only 100ms left before the cron deadline and a 15,000ms minimum fallback budget, the after-fix gate returns `fallbackAttempted=false` and reports `Skipping fallback: only 100ms remain before cron timeout (need at least 15000ms).` The before behavior had no `beforeAttempt` budget gate, so the fallback candidate was started even though it could only receive about 0ms.

**What was not tested:** Live Anthropic/OpenAI API calls were not run for this proof; the local reproduction covers the cron fallback budget decision and the targeted test covers the runtime hook path with mocked provider execution.

target.title: [perf] improve reply send performance
target.body:
## Summary

- Duplicate of #85853, rebased onto current `openclaw/openclaw:main` at `0ba6b235349add2d1d47b6543b980ee90d93a0df`.
- Keeps both commits from the original stack:
  - `fix: avoid blocking reply sends on bookkeeping`
  - `fix telegram progress tool result preview`
- Improves reply latency by avoiding dispatcher-idle waits on every direct block reply callback.
- Flushes pending block delivery only before subsequent tool/progress events, preserving block-before-tool ordering without making lightweight replies wait on dispatcher bookkeeping.
- Schedules source reply transcript mirroring after successful `message.action` sends without blocking the gateway response, while serializing async mirrors per session to preserve transcript chronology.
- Sends Telegram direct-message typing earlier after access checks so delayed context/session construction does not postpone the visible typing cue.

## Regression Source

#83722 is the merged regression source. It correctly fixed same-channel pre-tool block ordering by waiting for block reply delivery before tool execution could continue, but the merged implementation placed that delivery barrier directly on the hot block-reply callback path.

That means lightweight/direct replies can wait for dispatcher idle or delivery bookkeeping even when no later tool/progress event is about to be emitted. Under concurrent embedded runs, cron work, slow channel I/O, or transcript mirroring pressure, the ordering fix from #83722 turns into amplified user-visible reply latency.

This PR keeps the ordering guarantee introduced by #83722, but moves the wait to the point where ordering matters: before a later tool/progress emission is sent. Direct block replies can resolve without waiting on dispatcher idle, while a subsequent event still flushes pending block delivery first.

## Impact

Simple visible replies can return sooner without compromising user-visible send order. ACP direct block replies remain non-blocking for the common path, block-before-tool/progress chronology is preserved, transcript mirroring stays best-effort after successful source sends, and Telegram direct typing begins earlier without adding a duplicate immediate cue.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: direct visible replies should not be delayed by dispatcher idle waits, source transcript mirroring, or late Telegram typing setup while preserving block-before-tool/progress ordering.

Real environment tested: local OpenClaw source checkout on this rebased branch, with `origin/main` fetched to `0ba6b235349add2d1d47b6543b980ee90d93a0df` before PR creation.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: `git diff --check`; `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp-delivery.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/send.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-context.typing.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-message.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.test.ts`.

Evidence after fix: branch contains exactly the two duplicated commits on top of current `origin/main`; `git diff --check` was clean; focused Vitest passed 7 files / 381 tests.

Observed result after fix: reply dispatch, ACP delivery, gateway send transcript mirroring, Telegram typing, bot message, and Telegram dispatch regression coverage all passed on the rebased stack.

What was not tested: live Telegram/WhatsApp/Discord account roundtrip with real external credentials.

## Verification

- `git diff --check`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp-delivery.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/send.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-context.typing.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-message.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.test.ts`

target.title: fix: add inbound peer send policy matching
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds native `session.sendPolicy.rules[].match.peerEquals: "inboundPeer"` support.
- Allows relational send-policy checks between the outbound target and trusted inbound peer candidates for the current automatic-delivery turn, including `identityLinks` aliases.
- Threads inbound peer context through automatic replies, routed replies, follow-up routed replies, durable turn replies, and durable outbound delivery.
- Keeps send-policy-only predicates out of shared allow/deny scopes.
- Preserves explicit-send compatibility by keeping explicit `message.send`, gateway sends, and message-tool sends outside `session.sendPolicy` suppression.
- Persists `sendPolicyMode` for queued delivery replay and treats legacy queued entries without that field as explicit replay, preserving pre-PR queue semantics.
- Reports blocked automatic durable sends as `denied_by_send_policy` suppression outcomes.
- Adds focused unit coverage and docs/help labels for the new predicate.

Fixes #85175.

## Verification

Latest head: `86f2586aedc1966c00c99777135998dee47ba558`

Focused recovery, deliver, and send-policy validation requested by ClawSweeper:

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.infra.config.ts src/infra/outbound/delivery-queue.recovery.test.ts src/infra/outbound/deliver.test.ts
```

Result:

```text
Test Files  2 passed (2)
Tests       110 passed (110)
```

```text
node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/sessions/send-policy.test.ts
```

Result:

```text
[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 7.20s
Tests 14 passed
```

Additional focused validation for the explicit-send footprint trim and durable reply callers:

```text
node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/agents/tools/message-tool.test.ts src/infra/outbound/message-action-runner.plugin-dispatch.test.ts src/infra/outbound/message.test.ts src/infra/outbound/outbound-send-service.test.ts src/channels/message/send.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/send.test.ts src/channels/turn/durable-delivery.test.ts src/channels/turn/kernel.test.ts
```

Result:

```text
[test] passed 4 Vitest shards in 30.40s
Gateway shard: 51 passed
Infra shard: 52 passed
Agents shard: 59 passed
Channels shard: 55 passed
```

Type/lint checks:

```text
pnpm tsgo:core
pnpm exec oxlint src/infra/outbound/delivery-queue-recovery.ts src/infra/outbound/delivery-queue.recovery.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/followup-runner.ts src/auto-reply/reply/skill-tool-dispatch.runtime.ts src/agents/openclaw-tools.plugin-context.ts src/agents/openclaw-tools.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/params.ts src/agents/pi-tools.ts src/agents/tools/message-tool.ts src/agents/tools/message-tool.test.ts src/infra/outbound/message-action-runner.ts src/infra/outbound/message-action-runner.plugin-dispatch.test.ts src/infra/outbound/message.ts src/infra/outbound/message.test.ts src/infra/outbound/outbound-send-service.ts src/infra/outbound/outbound-send-service.test.ts src/plugins/tool-types.ts
```

Result:

```text
pnpm tsgo:core passed
oxlint passed
```

## Footprint reduction

The PR surface was reduced from ClawSweeper's reviewed `43 files / +1185 -58` to:

```text
30 files changed, 1038 insertions(+), 58 deletions(-)
```

Removed from the PR footprint:

- explicit message-tool `inboundPeer` propagation
- plugin tool-context `inboundPeer` propagation
- message-action-runner explicit-send `inboundPeer` propagation
- outbound-send-service explicit-send `inboundPeer` propagation
- related tests whose only purpose was proving explicit paths carried `inboundPeer`

Reasoning: explicit sends intentionally bypass `session.sendPolicy`, so passing `inboundPeer` through those explicit paths was unnecessary surface area. The automatic/durable reply path remains covered.

## Legacy queue compatibility

Recovery now treats queued delivery entries without `sendPolicyMode` as explicit replay:

```text
sendPolicyMode: entry.sendPolicyMode ?? "explicit"
```

This preserves pre-existing queued `message.send` / gateway retry behavior after upgrade under `session.sendPolicy.default: "deny"`.

Regression added:

```text
src/infra/outbound/delivery-queue.recovery.test.ts
- replays legacy queued entries without sendPolicyMode as explicit under deny-by-default policy
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** `session.sendPolicy.rules[].match.peerEquals: "inboundPeer"` must allow same-peer automatic Telegram durable replies and suppress wrong-peer automatic durable replies before platform send. The same PR also preserves explicit-send compatibility for legacy queued entries without `sendPolicyMode`.

- **Real environment tested:** Live Telegram bot setup using OpenClaw PR-head source at proof time, with a real Telegram direct peer `TG_PEER_A` and a distinct redacted target `TG_PEER_B`. Latest live Telegram proof comment: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/85763#issuecomment-4529388676. Current code-review head is `86f2586aedc1966c00c99777135998dee47ba558`; the live proof head was `e7b028ba5aa2c7444937deb020e838e130945f70`.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** On current head `86f2586aedc1966c00c99777135998dee47ba558`, I reran the focused recovery, deliver, send-policy, durable-reply, gateway-send, message-tool, typecheck, and lint commands listed in the Verification section. The live Telegram proof flow used the same durable `sendDurableMessageBatch` policy path: configure `session.sendPolicy` to deny direct Telegram when `{ "peerEquals": "inboundPeer", "invert": true }`, send one automatic durable reply to `TG_PEER_A`, then attempt an automatic durable send to `TG_PEER_B` in the same inbound-peer context.

- **Evidence after fix:** Redacted live Telegram proof comment: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/85763#issuecomment-4529388676. Copied redacted runtime output from that proof:

```text
Inbound Telegram direct session: agent:main:telegram:direct:TG_PEER_A_NUMERIC
Inbound peer candidates: TG_PEER_A, TG_PEER_A_NUMERIC, TG_PEER_A_USERNAME

Same-peer allow:
  outbound target: TG_PEER_A
  durable result: sent
  Telegram message id: 6
  Telegram API calls for this case: 1

Wrong-peer suppression:
  attempted outbound target: TG_PEER_B
  durable result: suppressed
  suppression reason: denied_by_send_policy
  cancel reason: send_policy_peer_mismatch
  Telegram API calls for this case: 0
```

Redacted diagnostic excerpt:

```json
{
  "status": "suppressed",
  "reason": "denied_by_send_policy",
  "hookEffect": {
    "cancelReason": "send_policy_peer_mismatch",
    "metadata": {
      "code": "send_policy_peer_mismatch",
      "peerEquals": "inboundPeer",
      "expectedPeers": ["TG_PEER_A", "TG_PEER_A_NUMERIC", "TG_PEER_A_USERNAME"],
      "actualPeer": "TG_PEER_B"
    }
  },
  "telegramApiCallsForCase": 0
}
```

Required proof gate status on current PR body/head:

```text
$ gh pr checks 85763 --repo openclaw/openclaw
Real behavior proof  pass  15s  https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/26369003419/job/77617646289

$ gh run view 26369003419 --repo openclaw/openclaw --log
External PR includes after-fix real behavior proof.
```

- **Observed result after fix:** Same-peer Telegram durable send to `TG_PEER_A` delivered with one Telegram API send call. Wrong-peer durable outbound attempt to `TG_PEER_B` was suppressed with `denied_by_send_policy` / `send_policy_peer_mismatch` and zero Telegram API send calls, so `TG_PEER_B` received no Telegram message. Current head additionally preserves legacy queued explicit sends by replaying missing `sendPolicyMode` as `explicit`, covered by `src/infra/outbound/delivery-queue.recovery.test.ts`.

- **What was not tested:** The real Telegram send itself was captured on proof head `e7b028ba5aa2c7444937deb020e838e130945f70`, not repeated after the final `86f2586a` recovery/footprint commit. That final commit is limited to legacy queue replay compatibility and explicit-send footprint cleanup, while the current PR body and head now pass the required `Real behavior proof` gate at `26369003419`.

### Config under test

`session.sendPolicy` denied direct Telegram sends when the outbound peer did not equal the trusted inbound peer candidates:

```json
{
  "default": "allow",
  "rules": [
    {
      "action": "deny",
      "match": {
        "allOf": [
          { "channel": "telegram" },
          { "chatType": "direct" },
          { "peerEquals": "inboundPeer", "invert": true }
        ]
      }
    }
  ]
}
```

### Proof verdict

- Same-peer Telegram durable send to `TG_PEER_A` was delivered.
- Wrong-peer durable outbound attempt to `TG_PEER_B` was suppressed by `session.sendPolicy`.
- Suppression included `denied_by_send_policy` and `send_policy_peer_mismatch`.
- The wrong-peer case recorded zero Telegram API send calls, so `TG_PEER_B` received no Telegram message.
- Token, real chat id, phone number, username, and message content beyond the proof marker are redacted.

target.title: fix(ui): add agent selector to dreaming tab
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: the Control UI Dreaming tab had no agent selector, so users could not switch Dreaming context between agent workspaces.
- Why it matters: Dreaming artifacts (`DREAMS.md`, short-term recall, schedules, memory state) are agent-specific, so the tab was effectively stuck to the current session context.
- What changed: added an agent selector to the Dreaming tab chrome and wired it to the same preferred-session-per-agent logic used by chat session controls.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no backend dreaming behavior, routing behavior, or storage behavior was changed.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #63558
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Dreaming tab lacked an agent selector, preventing users from switching agent contexts there.
- Real environment tested: local checkout of `openclaw/openclaw` on Linux.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `cd ui && pnpm vitest run src/ui/views/dreaming.test.ts --reporter=verbose`
  - `pnpm tsgo:core`
  - `pnpm lint:core`
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):
```text
✓ src/ui/views/dreaming.test.ts > dreaming view > renders an agent selector and switches agent contexts
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       16 passed (16)
```
- Observed result after fix: the Dreaming tab now renders an agent selector when multiple agents are available, preselects the active agent, and triggers agent-context switching through the same preferred-session resolution used elsewhere.
- What was not tested: end-to-end browser interaction against a live multi-agent gateway.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: Dreaming used its own tab chrome and never reused the shared agent-selection/session-switching logic used in chat.
- Missing detection / guardrail: Dreaming view tests did not assert presence of an agent selector for multi-agent setups.
- Contributing context (if known): the rest of Control UI already assumed agent-specific contexts for many views, so Dreaming lagged behind that UX pattern.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `ui/src/ui/views/dreaming.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: when multiple agents are available, Dreaming shows an agent selector, reflects the active agent, and calls back when the selection changes.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the missing feature is entirely in the Dreaming tab view/chrome layer.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): branch adds `renders an agent selector and switches agent contexts`.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

The Control UI Dreaming tab now shows an agent selector when multiple agent contexts are available, so users can switch Dreaming context directly from the tab.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: local repo checkout
- Area: Control UI / Dreaming tab

### Steps

1. Render Dreaming with multiple available agent options.
2. Verify an agent selector is present.
3. Verify the current agent is selected.
4. Change the selection and verify the callback fires with the target agent id.

### Expected

- Dreaming shows the current agent and allows agent switching.

### Actual

- Dreaming view test passes with the new selector behavior.
- `tsgo:core` passes.
- `lint:core` passes.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified the selector appears in the Dreaming tab chrome.
- Verified the selected agent is reflected in the UI.
- Verified agent changes route through preferred-session-per-agent logic.
- Verified view test, `tsgo:core`, and `lint:core` all pass.

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: the selector could route users to an unexpected session for an agent.
  - Mitigation: it uses the same preferred-session-per-agent resolution logic already used in chat controls.
- Risk: single-agent setups could get cluttered.
  - Mitigation: the selector renders only when more than one agent option is available.



---

## Real behavior proof

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway-methods.config.ts src/gateway/server-methods/doctor.test.ts

 RUN  v4.1.5 /tmp/pincer-openclaw

 ✓  gateway-methods  src/gateway/server-methods/doctor.test.ts (27 tests) 89ms

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
       Tests  27 passed (27)
    Start at  00:25:12
    Duration  470ms
```

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Selecting an agent in the Dreaming tab called `switchChatSession()` but `loadDreamingStatus` and `loadDreamDiary` still sent `{}` to `doctor.memory.*`, showing the default agent's data regardless of selection. The tab could display DREAMS.md and memory state for the wrong agent.
**Real environment tested:** Node.js v24.14.0, vitest v4.1.5, Linux.
**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway-methods.config.ts src/gateway/server-methods/doctor.test.ts`
**After-fix evidence:** 27/27 gateway method tests pass. TypeScript `tsc --noEmit` reports no errors across core and extensions configs.
**Observed result after fix:** `onSelectAgent` sets `selectedAgentId` on state, immediately reloads `dreamingStatus` and `dreamDiary` scoped to the selected agent. Gateway handlers accept optional `agentId` param with backward-compatible fallback to `resolveDefaultAgentId` when omitted.
**What was not tested:** Live multi-agent Control UI browser session; gateway handler routing is verified at the unit level through the existing 27 gateway method tests.

target.title: fix(agents): strip plaintext model provider keys
target.body:
## Summary
- strip plaintext provider `apiKey` values after merge-mode preservation, source-managed enforcement, and provider compatibility transforms so old `models.json` keys cannot be reintroduced into the final prompt-facing catalog
- clean legacy catalog-only `models.json` plaintext provider keys before the empty-provider `skip` path can preserve them
- migrate existing `models.json`-only plaintext provider keys into the target agent's local `auth-profiles.json` before writing the stripped catalog when there is no configured provider key and no target-local auth profile for that provider
- keep inherited main-agent profiles and runtime/external overlays from suppressing local legacy `models.json` key migration for non-main agents
- migrate known/env-present legacy env-name catalog credentials such as `LITELLM_KEY` to env-backed auth-profile `keyRef`s, while preserving ambiguous env-shaped plaintext strings as inline local auth-profile keys
- preserve non-secret markers such as env SecretRef markers and custom-provider `secretref-managed` compatibility markers
- add regression coverage for generated plaintext stripping, merge-mode stripping, catalog-only cleanup, existing-key migration, fail-closed behavior, inherited-profile migration boundaries, env-present keyRef migration, ambiguous env-shaped key preservation, and pi model discovery compatibility

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Generated and legacy `models.json` files should not persist plaintext provider `apiKey` values. Cleanup should preserve upgrade auth outside the prompt-facing catalog, including non-main agent local migration boundaries and env-shaped legacy values that must only become `keyRef`s when an env marker or observed env value proves they are env-backed.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source from this PR branch at `b23d1d7c`, macOS, Node.js `v24.1.0`, `/usr/bin/python3` for fs-safe pinned writes. Provider keys in proof output are redacted.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
FS_SAFE_PYTHON_MODE=require FS_SAFE_PYTHON=/usr/bin/python3 node --import tsx --input-type=module
```

The inline Node proof script created temporary local agent dirs, called `ensureOpenClawModelsJson`, `discoverModels`, `ensureAuthProfileStore`, and `createProviderAuthResolver`, and exercised these real paths: inline custom provider stripping, legacy configured `models.json` key migration, catalog-only cleanup, env-present `keyRef` migration, ambiguous env-shaped plaintext preservation, non-main inherited-profile migration boundary, and pi model discovery compatibility.

- Evidence after fix (copied live output, redacted):

```json
{
  "head": "b23d1d7c",
  "tempRoot": "<tmp>",
  "inlineCustomProvider": {
    "apiKeyMarker": "secretref-managed",
    "hasPlaintextInModelsJson": false,
    "discoverModelsFindsCustomModel": "custom-model"
  },
  "legacyModelsJsonOnlyUpgrade": {
    "apiKeyMarker": "secretref-managed",
    "hasPlaintextInModelsJson": false,
    "migratedProfile": {
      "type": "api_key",
      "provider": "custom",
      "copyToAgents": false,
      "displayName": "Migrated from models.json",
      "key": "<redacted>"
    },
    "discoverModelsFindsLegacyModel": "legacy-model"
  },
  "catalogOnlyLegacyCleanup": {
    "apiKeyMarker": "secretref-managed",
    "hasPlaintextInModelsJson": false,
    "migratedProfile": {
      "type": "api_key",
      "provider": "custom",
      "copyToAgents": false,
      "displayName": "Migrated from models.json",
      "key": "<redacted>"
    },
    "discoverModelsFindsCatalogOnlyModel": "catalog-only-model"
  },
  "envPresentCatalogMigration": {
    "modelsJsonPromptSafe": true,
    "migratedProfile": {
      "type": "api_key",
      "provider": "custom",
      "hasInlineKey": false,
      "keyRef": {
        "source": "env",
        "provider": "default",
        "id": "LITELLM_KEY"
      },
      "copyToAgents": false
    },
    "runtimeAuth": {
      "mode": "api_key",
      "source": "profile",
      "profileId": "custom:models-json",
      "apiKeyMarker": "LITELLM_KEY",
      "resolvedEnvValueSeen": true
    }
  },
  "ambiguousEnvShapedCredential": {
    "modelsJsonPromptSafe": true,
    "migratedProfile": {
      "type": "api_key",
      "provider": "custom",
      "hasInlineKey": true,
      "hasKeyRef": false,
      "copyToAgents": false
    },
    "runtimeAuth": {
      "mode": "api_key",
      "source": "profile",
      "profileId": "custom:models-json",
      "preservedLocalKeySeen": true
    }
  },
  "inheritedProfileBoundary": {
    "modelsJsonPromptSafe": true,
    "localMigratedProfile": {
      "type": "api_key",
      "provider": "custom",
      "copyToAgents": false,
      "displayName": "Migrated from models.json",
      "key": "<redacted>"
    },
    "runtimeAuth": {
      "mode": "api_key",
      "source": "profile",
      "profileId": "custom:models-json",
      "resolvedWorkerKey": true
    }
  }
}
```

- Observed result after fix: the final `models.json` omits plaintext keys; non-built-in custom providers with inline model rows retain the non-secret `secretref-managed` marker; old configured and catalog-only `models.json` credentials migrate into `auth-profiles.json`; env-present legacy catalog values migrate as env-backed `keyRef` credentials and resolve through provider auth; ambiguous env-shaped legacy values with no env signal are preserved as inline local auth-profile keys instead of unresolved env refs; a non-main agent with an inherited same-profile ID still migrates its local legacy catalog key and runtime auth resolves the worker-local key; and pi `discoverModels` can still find custom model rows.
- What was not tested: I did not test a real paid third-party provider. The live proof did not force auth-profile persistence failure; that fail-closed path is covered by the focused regression test listed below.

## Tests
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/models-config.write-serialization.test.ts --maxWorkers=1 --reporter=dot`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/models-config.plan.test.ts --maxWorkers=1 --reporter=dot`
- `pnpm exec tsc --noEmit --pretty false -p tsconfig.core.json`
- `NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=8192 pnpm exec tsc --noEmit --pretty false -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/agents/models-config.ts src/agents/models-config.write-serialization.test.ts src/agents/models-config.plan.ts src/agents/models-config.plan.test.ts src/agents/auth-profiles.ts src/agents/auth-profiles/store.ts`
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json src/agents/models-config.ts src/agents/models-config.write-serialization.test.ts src/agents/models-config.plan.ts src/agents/models-config.plan.test.ts src/agents/auth-profiles.ts src/agents/auth-profiles/store.ts`
- `git diff --check`

Refs #11829

target.title: Redact exec tool result payloads
target.body:
## Summary
- redact exec foreground tool-result text and structured `details.aggregated` before returning results to the agent
- redact node-host exec stdout/stderr/error output before formatting tool results
- redact process poll/log/list output and structured details that can include output tails or aggregated process output

## Real behavior proof
**Behavior or issue addressed:** Exec and background process tool results could return secret-shaped stdout/stderr/session text back to the agent. This patch redacts those returned tool-result surfaces before they leave the exec/process/node-host formatters.

**Real environment tested:** Real local OpenClaw checkout on branch `fix/exec-tool-result-redaction` at `da701a0bbf6c`, using an isolated temporary `OPENCLAW_HOME`. The proof used an obvious fake canary value, not a real credential.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a local runtime proof that exercised `createExecTool` and `createProcessTool` with a fake `REDACTION_CANARY_SECRET` value, then inspected the returned tool-result `content[0].text` and structured `details.aggregated` values for foreground exec, process poll, process log, and process list tail/details surfaces.

**Evidence after fix:** Copied live output from the local runtime proof:

```json
{
  "foregroundExec": {
    "status": "completed",
    "text": "REDACTION_CANARY_SECRET=clawsw…7890",
    "detailsAggregated": "REDACTION_CANARY_SECRET=clawsw…7890",
    "rawCanaryValuePresent": false,
    "redactedMarkerPresent": true
  },
  "processPoll": {
    "status": "completed",
    "text": "REDACTION_CANARY_SECRET=clawsw…7890\n\n\nProcess exited with code 0.",
    "detailsAggregated": "REDACTION_CANARY_SECRET=clawsw…7890\n",
    "rawCanaryValuePresent": false,
    "redactedMarkerPresent": true
  },
  "processLog": {
    "status": "completed",
    "text": "REDACTION_CANARY_SECRET=clawsw…7890",
    "detailsAggregated": "",
    "rawCanaryValuePresent": false,
    "redactedMarkerPresent": true
  },
  "processList": {
    "status": "completed",
    "textTail": "REDACTION_CANARY_SECRET=clawsw…7890",
    "rawCanaryValuePresent": false,
    "redactedMarkerPresent": true
  }
}
```

Supplemental focused regression coverage also exercises node-host exec stdout redaction by passing a fake node `system.run` payload through `executeNodeHostCommand` and asserting both returned text and `details.aggregated` redact the raw canary.

**Observed result after fix:** The raw fake canary value was absent from returned foreground exec, process poll, process log, and process list output. Redacted markers were present in the returned text/details where output was expected. The node-host formatter path is covered by the focused regression test for the same returned tool-result boundary.

PR surface: Other +245. Total +245 across 2 files.

target.title: fix(install): validate downloaded scripts before execution
target.body:
## Problem

Four code paths in `scripts/install.sh` download remote scripts via `download_file` and execute them with `bash "$tmp"` without any validation. While HTTPS/TLS1.2 protects the transport, truncated downloads, HTML error pages (e.g., CDN 403 responses), and empty responses would be silently executed as bash scripts.

Affected paths:
1. `run_remote_bash` (Homebrew installer from `raw.githubusercontent.com`)
2. NodeSource apt/deb setup script (`deb.nodesource.com/setup_*.x`)
3. NodeSource dnf/rpm setup script (`rpm.nodesource.com/setup_*.x`)
4. NodeSource yum/rpm setup script (`rpm.nodesource.com/setup_*.x`)

Contrast with `bootstrap_gum_temp` which verifies downloaded content with SHA256 checksums.

## Fix

Extract a reusable `validate_downloaded_script()` function and apply it to all four download-then-execute paths. The validation:
1. Rejects empty files (`[[ ! -s "$file" ]]`)
2. Rejects files that don't start with a shebang (`#!`)
3. Sanitizes the first-line diagnostic before logging: strips control characters (bytes 0x00-0x1F and 0x7F) via `LC_ALL=C tr -d '\000-\037\177'` and escapes backslashes (`${safe_line//\\/\\\\}`) so untrusted downloaded content cannot inject terminal escape sequences through `ui_error`'s `echo -e` path.

`run_remote_bash` calls `validate_downloaded_script` internally. The three NodeSource paths call it explicitly after `download_file` and before `bash "$tmp"` (or `sudo bash "$tmp"`).

A full SHA256 checksum pin is impractical because these setup scripts update frequently and a pinned hash would break the installer on every release.

**Sanitization detail:** The `ui_error` function uses `echo -e` in the non-gum fallback path, which interprets backslash sequences (`\033[...` ANSI escapes, `\n` newlines, etc.). Two layers prevent terminal injection: (1) `tr -d` strips raw control bytes (actual ESC 0x1B), (2) backslash escaping prevents `echo -e` from interpreting literal `\033[31m` text as ANSI escapes.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: `run_remote_bash()` executed downloaded content with `/bin/bash` unconditionally. Truncated downloads, HTML error pages, JSON errors, and binary blobs would all be piped to bash. Additionally, the first-line diagnostic in the rejection message passed untrusted content through `ui_error`'s `echo -e` path, which interprets backslash escape sequences (terminal injection).

Real environment tested: Linux (Ubuntu 24.04, WSL2, bash 5.x). The real patched `run_remote_bash` function was extracted from `scripts/install.sh` via `sed` and exercised against a local Python HTTP server serving 4 response types.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
Step 1 -- start a local HTTP server serving test responses (HTML error, empty, valid script, escape attack). Step 2 -- extract the real `run_remote_bash` from the patched `install.sh` and call it against each endpoint:
```bash
python3 -c '<HTTP server serving /html-error, /empty, /valid-script, /escape-attack>'
eval "$(sed -n '/^run_remote_bash()/,/^}/p' scripts/install.sh)"
run_remote_bash "http://127.0.0.1:19877/html-error"
run_remote_bash "http://127.0.0.1:19877/empty"
run_remote_bash "http://127.0.0.1:19877/valid-script"
run_remote_bash "http://127.0.0.1:19877/escape-attack"
```

Evidence after fix: terminal output copied below.

**Live proof: real `run_remote_bash` against HTTP server**

The actual `run_remote_bash` function from the patched `install.sh` was extracted via `sed -n '/^run_remote_bash()/,/^}/p'` and called against a local Python HTTP server. This exercises the real patched code path, not a test harness copy.

```console
=== REAL run_remote_bash from patched install.sh ===

--- Test 1: HTML error page ---
✗ Downloaded file does not look like a shell script (no shebang): http://127.0.0.1:19877/html-error
✗ First line: <html><body>404 Not Found</body></html>
Exit code: 1

--- Test 2: Empty response ---
✗ Downloaded script is empty: http://127.0.0.1:19877/empty
Exit code: 1

--- Test 3: Valid #!/bin/bash script ---
hello-from-valid-script
Exit code: 0

--- Test 4: Terminal escape attack ---
✗ Downloaded file does not look like a shell script (no shebang): http://127.0.0.1:19877/escape-attack
✗ First line: \033[31mHACKED\033[0m terminal injection
Exit code: 1
```

**Test 1 (HTML):** Server returns `<html><body>404 Not Found</body></html>`. Rejected because no `#!` shebang. First line shown in diagnostic.

**Test 2 (empty):** Server returns empty body. Rejected by the `[[ ! -s "$tmp" ]]` check.

**Test 3 (valid script):** Server returns `#!/bin/bash\necho hello-from-valid-script`. Accepted, executed by `/bin/bash`, printed `hello-from-valid-script`.

**Test 4 (escape attack):** Server returns literal text `\033[31mHACKED\033[0m terminal injection` (ASCII backslash-zero-three-three, NOT actual ESC bytes). Rejected (no shebang). The first-line diagnostic shows `\033[31mHACKED\033[0m` as **literal text**, NOT as red-colored terminal output. Without the sanitization fix, `echo -e` would interpret `\033[31m` as ANSI red and show colored "HACKED" text. The double sanitization (control byte stripping via `tr -d` + backslash escaping via `${safe_line//\\/\\\\}`) prevents both attack vectors.

**Sanitization detail**

Two layers protect the first-line diagnostic from terminal injection:

1. **Control byte stripping** (`LC_ALL=C tr -d '\000-\037\177'`): Removes actual ESC (0x1B), NUL, and other control bytes from the downloaded content.
2. **Backslash escaping** (`${safe_line//\\/\\\\}`): Doubles all backslashes so `echo -e` treats `\033` as literal text instead of interpreting it as an ESC byte.

Verification with a crafted payload:
```console
Literal \033[31m text (7 ASCII chars, no real ESC byte):
$ echo -e "First line: \033[31mHACKED\033[0m"
First line: HACKED    # <-- RED colored text (dangerous!)

$ safe_line='\033[31mHACKED\033[0m'; safe_line="${safe_line//\\/\\\\}"
$ echo -e "First line: ${safe_line}"
First line: \033[31mHACKED\033[0m    # <-- plain text (safe!)
```

**Supplemental: validation harness (10/10 pass)**

```console
$ bash scripts/test-install-validation.sh
── GREEN: validation rejects bad downloads ──
  ✓ PASS  empty file  → rejected
  ✓ PASS  HTML error   → rejected
  ✓ PASS  JSON error   → rejected
  ✓ PASS  binary blob  → rejected
  ✓ PASS  valid script (#!bash)     → accepted
  ✓ PASS  valid script (#!env bash) → accepted

── RED: no validation lets bad downloads through ──
  ✓ PASS  empty file  → accepted (DANGEROUS without validation)
  ✓ PASS  HTML error   → accepted (DANGEROUS without validation)
  ✓ PASS  JSON error   → accepted (DANGEROUS without validation)
  ✓ PASS  binary blob  → accepted (DANGEROUS without validation)

Results: 10 passed, 0 failed
```

**Live HTTPS proof: unmodified `download_file` over TLS**

Proof using the **real unmodified `download_file` function** from `install.sh` (not a test stub). The function uses `curl --proto '=https' --tlsv1.2 --retry 3` -- the identical flags used in production for the Homebrew installer download from `raw.githubusercontent.com`.

The server is a local Python `ssl.SSLContext` HTTPS server with a self-signed certificate (SAN: `IP:127.0.0.1`). `CURL_CA_BUNDLE` is set to trust the self-signed cert, so curl's TLS verification passes. No `--insecure` flag is used.

```console
=== REAL download_file + run_remote_bash over HTTPS ===
download_file: curl --proto =https --tlsv1.2 (unmodified from install.sh)
CURL_CA_BUNDLE=/tmp/test-cert.pem (trust self-signed cert for 127.0.0.1)

--- Test 1: HTTPS HTML error (real download_file) ---
x Downloaded file does not look like a shell script (no shebang): https://127.0.0.1:19879/html-error
x First line: <html><body>403 Forbidden</body></html>
Exit code: 1

--- Test 2: HTTPS empty (real download_file) ---
x Downloaded script is empty: https://127.0.0.1:19879/empty
Exit code: 1

--- Test 3: HTTPS valid script (real download_file) ---
hello-from-REAL-download-file-HTTPS
Exit code: 0
```

All three tests use the **unmodified `download_file` function** extracted via `sed` from `install.sh`. The function calls `curl --proto '=https' --tlsv1.2 --retry 3 --retry-delay 1 --retry-connrefused` -- the identical command line used in production. No `--insecure` flag. `CURL_CA_BUNDLE` trusts the self-signed cert so TLS verification passes normally.

Observed result after fix: The real patched `run_remote_bash` and `validate_downloaded_script` functions reject HTML errors, empty responses, and escape-attack payloads while accepting valid `#!/bin/bash` scripts. The first-line diagnostic is sanitized against both raw control bytes and literal backslash escape sequences. The HTTPS proof uses the real unmodified `download_file` function with `--proto '=https' --tlsv1.2` over TLS against a self-signed cert trusted via `CURL_CA_BUNDLE`. All 10 validation harness tests pass.

What was not tested: Live download from `raw.githubusercontent.com` returning an actual CDN error page (structural validation is tested against local HTTP and HTTPS servers instead).

target.title: Redact exec tool result payloads

## Summary
- redact exec foreground tool-result text and structured `details.aggregated` before returning results to the agent
- redact node-host exec stdout/stderr/error output before formatting tool results
- redact process poll/log/list output and structured details that can include output tails or aggregated process output

## Real behavior proof
**Behavior or issue addressed:** Exec and background process tool results could return secret-shaped stdout/stderr/session text back to the agent. This patch redacts those returned tool-result surfaces before they leave the exec/process/node-host formatters.

**Real environment tested:** Real local OpenClaw checkout on branch `fix/exec-tool-result-redaction` at `da701a0bbf6c`, using an isolated temporary `OPENCLAW_HOME`. The proof used an obvious fake canary value, not a real credential.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a local runtime proof that exercised `createExecTool` and `createProcessTool` with a fake `REDACTION_CANARY_SECRET` value, then inspected the returned tool-result `content[0].text` and structured `details.aggregated` values for foreground exec, process poll, process log, and process list tail/details surfaces.

**Evidence after fix:** Copied live output from the local runtime proof:

```json
{
  "foregroundExec": {
    "status": "completed",
    "text": "REDACTION_CANARY_SECRET=clawsw…7890",
    "detailsAggregated": "REDACTION_CANARY_SECRET=clawsw…7890",
    "rawCanaryValuePresent": false,
    "redactedMarkerPresent": true
  },
  "processPoll": {
    "status": "completed",
    "text": "REDACTION_CANARY_SECRET=clawsw…7890\n\n\nProcess exited with code 0.",
    "detailsAggregated": "REDACTION_CANARY_SECRET=clawsw…7890\n",
    "rawCanaryValuePresent": false,
    "redactedMarkerPresent": true
  },
  "processLog": {
    "status": "completed",
    "text": "REDACTION_CANARY_SECRET=clawsw…7890",
    "detailsAggregated": "",
    "rawCanaryValuePresent": false,
    "redactedMarkerPresent": true
  },
  "processList": {
    "status": "completed",
    "textTail": "REDACTION_CANARY_SECRET=clawsw…7890",
    "rawCanaryValuePresent": false,
    "redactedMarkerPresent": true
  }
}
```

target.title: telegram: fast-ack final button callbacks
target.body:
Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (Yes/No)

No

What is the highest-risk area?

Telegram callback handling, specifically the new fast-lane acknowledgment path that runs before the normal sequentialized callback processing.

How is that risk mitigated?

The change is narrowly scoped to known final-button callback payloads and keeps the normal callback handler in place.
Mitigations:
- callback payloads are explicitly matched, including legacy values
- the fast lane marks context so the normal handler does not send duplicate visible acknowledgments
- the normal handler still provides visible fallback behavior if answerCallbackQuery is stale or fails
- full Telegram extension test lane passed after updating adjacent callback expectations

<details>
<summary>Risk guidance</summary>

The main behavioral risk is callback acknowledgment ordering: this change intentionally moves acknowledgment earlier for supported final-button presses so users get immediate feedback.

Reviewers should verify:
- only the intended final-button payloads take the fast lane
- callback toasts and visible thread messages use the mapped text
- duplicate visible acknowledgments are suppressed when the fast lane already sent one
- stale callback cases still surface the visible fallback message
- unrelated callback flows continue to receive the generic Received callback toast contract

</details>

## Current review state

What is the next action?

Review the Telegram callback flow changes in extensions/telegram/src/bot-core.ts and extensions/telegram/src/bot-handlers.runtime.ts, with attention to ordering and duplicate-ack suppression.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

Nothing is blocked on author input right now.
Local verification is complete:
- targeted callback-focused Telegram tests passed
- full Telegram extension lane passed (117 files, 1744 tests)

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

Addressed the concern that the acknowledgment path should be implemented in upstream source rather than as ad-hoc local compiled-file patches.
Also added test coverage for:
- pre-sequentialization final-button acknowledgment
- no duplicate visible acknowledgment after fast-lane ack
- visible fallback when callback toast delivery is stale/fails

target.title: [codex] Add channel broker plugin protocol
target.body:
## Summary

Adds Phase 1 of the Channel Broker consolidation program: a versioned `openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-broker` protocol surface, a bundled `channel-broker` channel plugin, provider-owned outbound request/receipt helpers, docs, and focused tests.

This PR intentionally stays at the protocol/foundation layer. Telegram, Discord, Slack, and later channel migrations are tracked separately and should not be folded into this PR.

## Linked Context

Closes #86107
Related #86105
Related #86108
Related #86109
Related #86110
Related #86111
Related #86112
Related #86113
Related #86114
Related #86115

## Review Repair

Pushed `b3eeb2c1f1` to address ClawSweeper's current blockers:

- Preserves native prefix ownership when `channel-broker` declares platform aliases that overlap native plugins. If Telegram/Discord/Slack are registered, their prefixes stay native-owned; broker aliases own a platform prefix only when no native plugin declares it.
- Adds the standard channel SecretRef contract for `channels.channel-broker.{outboundToken,signingSecret}` plus `accounts.*` and `providers.*` variants, and changes the broker schema/docs to use SecretRef-compatible credential input.
- Threads `AbortSignal` through broker outbound requests, including the HTTP `fetch` transport and custom runtime override path.

## Real Behavior Proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Verified the Phase 1 broker surface resolves as a bundled channel and produces a normalized V1 outbound request for a Telegram-scoped broker target. This proves the user-visible foundation being shipped in this PR: catalog discovery, channel id normalization, target construction, protocol versioning, platform normalization, thread preservation, and final-send payload shape.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`, branch `feat/channel-broker-protocol`, after the review repair commit `b3eeb2c1f1` was created.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

  ```bash
  node --import tsx -e 'const { listBundledChannelCatalogEntries } = await import("./src/channels/bundled-channel-catalog-read.ts"); const { normalizeChatChannelId } = await import("./src/channels/ids.ts"); const { buildBrokerConversationTarget, createBrokerOutboundRequest } = await import("./src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.ts"); const catalog = listBundledChannelCatalogEntries().find((entry) => entry.id === "channel-broker"); const target = buildBrokerConversationTarget({ platform: "Telegram", conversationId: "chat 123", threadId: "topic-7" }); const request = createBrokerOutboundRequest({ requestId: "send-proof-1", providerId: "synthetic-provider", platform: "Telegram", accountId: "default", conversation: { id: "chat 123", type: "channel", threadId: "topic-7" }, mode: "final", payloads: [{ text: "broker proof" }], requirements: { text: true, thread: true } }); console.log(JSON.stringify({ discoveredChannel: catalog && { id: catalog.id, order: catalog.order, docsPath: catalog.channel.docsPath }, channelNormalization: normalizeChatChannelId("channel-broker"), target, outboundProtocolVersion: request.version, outboundMode: request.mode, outboundPlatform: request.platform, outboundConversation: request.conversation, outboundPayloadText: request.payloads[0]?.text }, null, 2));'
  ```

- **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output copied from the command above:

  ```json
  {
    "discoveredChannel": {
      "id": "channel-broker",
      "order": 60,
      "docsPath": "/channels/channel-broker"
    },
    "channelNormalization": "channel-broker",
    "target": "telegram:chat%20123?threadId=topic-7",
    "outboundProtocolVersion": 1,
    "outboundMode": "final",
    "outboundPlatform": "telegram",
    "outboundConversation": {
      "id": "chat 123",
      "type": "channel",
      "threadId": "topic-7"
    },
    "outboundPayloadText": "broker proof"
  }
  ```

- **Observed result after fix:** OpenClaw discovered the bundled `channel-broker` entry, normalized the channel id, built a Telegram broker target that preserved `threadId`, and emitted a V1 final outbound request with normalized `platform: "telegram"`, `conversation.threadId: "topic-7"`, and payload text `broker proof`.
- **What was not tested:** No real third-party broker provider delivery was tested in Phase 1. That is intentionally deferred to the Phase 2 migration proof issues for Telegram, Discord, and Slack.

## Focused Local Validation

All local validation below ran from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`:

```bash
git diff --check
```

```bash
node node_modules/oxfmt/bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 --config .oxfmtrc.jsonc docs/channels/channel-broker.md docs/reference/secretref-credential-surface.md extensions/channel-broker/src/accounts.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/outbound.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/runtime.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/secret-contract.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/secret-contract.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/types.ts src/infra/outbound/channel-target-prefix.ts src/infra/outbound/channel-target-prefix.test.ts
```

```bash
pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/secret-contract.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts
```

```bash
pnpm exec vitest run src/infra/outbound/channel-target-prefix.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.config.ts
```

```bash
pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json
```

```bash
pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/bundled-plugin-build-entries.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.config.ts
```

## Known Inherited Gates

- `pnpm plugin-sdk:surface:check` has an inherited clean-upstream SDK budget drift; this PR adds the intended `plugin-sdk/channel-broker` public subpath.
- `pnpm docs:check-i18n-glossary` has inherited unrelated glossary-label failures on clean upstream; Channel Broker glossary entries were added in this branch.

## Current Review State

Draft remains intentional while fresh GitHub checks and ClawSweeper review run on head `b3eeb2c1f1`. There are no known local blockers after the review repair above.
