target.title: fix(acp): persist spawn labels in target session store
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: `/acp spawn ... --label <name>` persisted the spawned ACP session metadata into the target agent store, but tried to persist the label into the requester store.
- Why it matters: Cross-agent ACP spawns (for example from a Telegram topic into a `codex` ACP session) silently lost the requested label, so follow-up commands like `/focus <label>` and `/acp status <label>` could not resolve the spawned session by label.
- What changed: `persistSpawnedSessionLabel(...)` now resolves the target session store from the spawned ACP `sessionKey` before writing the label, and only mutates the in-memory requester store when both stores are actually the same.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): ACP spawn routing, binding behavior, and runtime option handling are unchanged.
- AI-assisted: Yes. This PR was prepared with Codex.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- None

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

ACP sessions spawned with `/acp spawn ... --label <name>` now retain the requested label even when the spawned session is written to a different agent session store than the requester. Label-based follow-up commands such as `/focus <label>` and `/acp status <label>` now work in those cross-agent ACP flows.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (Yes/No) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (Yes/No) No
- New/changed network calls? (Yes/No) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (Yes/No) No
- Data access scope changed? (Yes/No) No
- If any Yes, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: local source install + local dev checkout
- Model/provider: ACPX (`codex` / `gemini` harnesses)
- Integration/channel (if any): Telegram ACP topic binding
- Relevant config (redacted): `acp.enabled=true`, `acp.backend=acpx`, `acp.allowedAgents=["codex","gemini"]`

### Steps

1. From a Telegram topic with ACP commands enabled, run `/acp spawn codex --bind here --label codex-a2ui --cwd /path/to/worktree`.
2. Observe that the spawned ACP session is created in the `codex` session store.
3. Try to resolve the session by label, for example with `/focus codex-a2ui` or `/acp status codex-a2ui`.

### Expected

- The spawned ACP session entry in the target agent store includes `label: "codex-a2ui"`.
- Label-based session resolution works for follow-up commands.

### Actual (before fix)

- The spawned ACP session entry in the target agent store had `label: null`.
- The label was written against the requester store instead, so label-based resolution failed.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets: local reproduction showed the spawned ACP session persisted in the target store with `label: null` before the fix; after patching the persistence path, the same session entry carried the requested label.
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: traced the spawn path, confirmed the label write used the requester `storePath`, reproduced the bug locally with a Telegram-bound ACP `codex` session, then verified the patched code writes to the target session store resolved from the spawned `sessionKey`.
- Edge cases checked: confirmed the in-memory requester store is only mutated when it matches the resolved target store, so same-store spawns still behave normally.
- What you did **not** verify: kept verification minimal per request. This checkout's current `origin/main` also has unrelated missing-dependency/typecheck failures (`@modelcontextprotocol/sdk`, `matrix-js-sdk`, and related surfaces), so `pnpm build` and the focused `pnpm vitest run src/auto-reply/reply/commands-acp.test.ts` run did not complete cleanly in this environment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (Yes/No) Yes
- Config/env changes? (Yes/No) No
- Migration needed? (Yes/No) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: revert this commit
- Files/config to restore: `src/auto-reply/reply/commands-acp/lifecycle.ts`, `src/auto-reply/reply/commands-acp.test.ts`
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: labels still missing on cross-agent ACP spawns, or label writes regressing for same-store spawns

## Risks and Mitigations

List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`.

- Risk: The fix touches session-store selection during spawn label persistence, so a bad store-path resolution could break label writes for a subset of ACP spawn flows.
  - Mitigation: Added a regression test that specifically covers a requester-store/target-store mismatch and asserts the label is written to the spawned session store, not the requester store.

target.title: feat(whatsapp): ACP session binding with media threading and prompt fixes
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: WhatsApp has no ACP (Agent Communication Protocol) session support — no session routing, no media threading to Claude Code, no plugin hook integration
- Why it matters: WhatsApp conversations cant leverage configured ACP bindings for agent routing; group history media is lost when reaching Claude Code sessions
- What changed:
  - Resolve configured ACP bindings in WhatsApp on-message handler
  - Thread group history `mediaPath`/`mediaType` through `InboundHistory` to ACP prompt
  - Resolve `<media:image>` placeholders to actual base64 content in ACP sessions
  - Strip `ThreadStarterBody` per turn to avoid repetition in long-lived sessions
  - Include `InboundHistory` in ACP prompt for group context continuity
  - Treat WhatsApp block replies as visible text in ACP delivery
  - Add `before_prompt_build` hook support with required `messages` field
  - Wire `memory-lancedb-pro` auto-recall through `before_prompt_build` hook
  - Add plugin lifecycle hooks dispatch to ACP flow
  - Expand ACP config schema with session routing fields
- What did NOT change: Non-ACP WhatsApp flow, other channel ACP bindings

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Feature
- [x] Bug fix

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts

## Linked Issue/PR

- Supersedes #56556 (closed for consolidation)

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

N/A — new feature with bug fixes to ACP dispatch

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level:
  - [x] Unit test
- Target test: `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.test.ts` (47 tests)
- Scenarios: ThreadStarterBody stripping, media placeholder resolution, InboundHistory threading, hook integration

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- WhatsApp conversations can now use configured ACP bindings for agent session routing
- Group history images reach Claude Code sessions as base64 content
- ACP sessions respect `before_prompt_build` plugin hooks

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
WhatsApp message flow with ACP:
[WhatsApp msg] -> on-message.ts
  -> resolveConfiguredBindingRoute() -> ensureConfiguredBindingRouteReady()
  -> processMessage() -> dispatchFromConfig()
    -> dispatch-acp.ts (with plugin hooks, media resolution, InboundHistory)
    -> Claude Code session
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No (uses existing ACP transport)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? Yes — group history media now forwarded to ACP sessions
- Mitigation: Only media already accessible in the conversation is forwarded

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? New optional ACP binding config keys in agents schema
- Migration needed? No

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Large base64 images in ACP prompt increase token usage
  - Mitigation: Only images with existing media placeholders are resolved; context pruning handles overflow

target.title: Feature Request: Add Feishu Interactive Card Parsing support #48281
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Feishu inbound `interactive` messages were parsed as `[Interactive Card]` only, so card header/elements/actions were not available to the agent.
- Why it matters: Blocks card-based workflows (weekly reports, approvals, form-like actions) because the bot cannot read card content.
- What changed: Added interactive card parsing for inbound events, fetches richer card payload via `raw_card_content`, extracts readable text, and forwards raw card JSON to context for advanced parsing.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No outbound card rendering/protocol changes, no new Feishu permissions, no changes to non-Feishu channels.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #48281
- Related #55466
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

- Root cause: Inbound Feishu `interactive` payloads were not parsed beyond placeholder text in the inbound event path.
- Missing detection / guardrail: No focused inbound interactive-card test asserting extracted content + raw card payload propagation.
- Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): Existing parsing focused on text/post + outbound card sending.
- Why this regressed now: N/A (feature gap / missing implementation rather than a recent regression).
- If unknown, what was ruled out: Confirmed issue is in inbound parse path, not outbound send path.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `extensions/feishu/src/bot.checkBotMentioned.test.ts`
  - `extensions/feishu/src/bot.test.ts`
  - `extensions/feishu/src/send.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: Inbound interactive card -> extracted readable text + raw card JSON available in inbound context (`RawBody`).
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: Covers both parser behavior and message-handling wiring without requiring live Feishu infra.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): `send.test.ts` had partial interactive extraction coverage; extended/leveraged.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A (new tests added).

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Incoming Feishu interactive cards are now readable by the bot (header + element text extraction).
- Inbound context now includes raw interactive card JSON for advanced card/action parsing.
- Placeholder-only behavior for interactive cards is no longer the default when card payload is available.

```text
Before:
[user sends Feishu interactive card] -> [bot sees "[Interactive Card]"] -> [cannot analyze real card content]

After:
[user sends Feishu interactive card] -> [fetch + parse full card payload] -> [text extracted + raw card JSON attached] -> [bot can analyze/report/action]

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`No`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`)
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes`)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`)
- Data access scope changed? (`No`)
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:
   Added targeted Feishu message fetch for inbound interactive messages to obtain richer card payload.
  Mitigation: Uses existing authenticated Feishu client and existing message scope; no new credentials/privileges introduced.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

OS: Windows (PowerShell)
Runtime/container: Node 22 + pnpm (corepack)
Model/provider: N/A
Integration/channel (if any): Feishu extension
Relevant config (redacted): channels.feishu.dmPolicy=open (test/mocked configs)

### Steps

1 Send an inbound Feishu interactive message/card to the bot.
2 Observe parsed inbound content/context in Feishu handling path.
3 Run targeted tests listed below.

### Expected

Card text content is extracted (header + elements).
Raw card JSON is available in context (RawBody) for advanced parsing.
-

### Actual

-Verified as expected; targeted tests pass.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
  Targeted tests pass for interactive parsing and context propagation:
  corepack pnpm test -- extensions/feishu/src/send.test.ts -t interactive
  corepack pnpm test -- extensions/feishu/src/bot.checkBotMentioned.test.ts -t interactive
  corepack pnpm test -- extensions/feishu/src/bot.test.ts -t "fetches full interactive card content and forwards raw card JSON in context"
  Type-check passed: corepack pnpm tsgo

- Edge cases checked:
  Placeholder fallback path
  Card header + mixed element extraction
  Raw card payload forwarding

- What you did **not** verify:
   Live Feishu tenant end-to-end in production workspace (manual real-tenant validation still recommended).

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`No`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: (N/A)

## Risks and Mitigations

Risk: Unexpected card schemas may still produce partial text extraction.
Mitigation: Conservative fallback to placeholder/raw content path and centralized parser for future schema extension.
Risk: Extra Feishu API call for inbound interactive messages can increase latency slightly.
Mitigation: Call is scoped to interactive type only and uses existing client/retry/error handling paths.

target.title: fix(codex): preserve semantic native threads across compaction
target.body:
## Summary

This is PR #4 in the Codex native-thread stack, after #85978, #86069, and #86094. It turns the diagnostics foundation into the long-term cache/lifecycle fix for compatible context-engine `thread_bootstrap` bindings:

- preserve matching Codex `thread_bootstrap` bindings across successful context-engine-owned compaction;
- copy preserved bindings to successor session files during transcript rollover, then clear the archived/original binding;
- reread the active Codex binding after forced compaction before rebuilding the Codex prompt;
- keep legacy, ownerless, non-bootstrap, mismatch, and app-server rejection paths rotating/clearing as before;
- add `context-engine-compaction-preserved-binding` lifecycle diagnostics and docs explaining the preserved vs invalidated paths.

## Why this is not “raise 70k to 258k/272k”

The default `70000` native reuse guard remains a legacy/stale-binding safety rail. The healthy Codex + LCM path is semantic ownership:

1. Context engine returns `contextProjection.mode = "thread_bootstrap"` with a stable epoch/fingerprint.
2. OpenClaw injects large assembled context once into the Codex native thread.
3. Later turns resume that native thread and send only the current prompt when the engine/policy/projection/tool identity still matches.
4. Compaction or rollover preserves the binding only when that semantic identity remains safe.
5. Actual app-server overflow/rejection still clears the binding before fresh-thread retry.

The important distinction is model capacity vs warm-thread lifecycle policy:

| Value | Meaning | Share of 70k guard |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `70000` | OpenClaw proactive native-thread reuse guard for legacy/stale bindings | 100% |
| `272000` | OpenClaw Codex runtime input cap used for GPT-5.5 forward-compat rows | 70k is 25.7% |
| `400000` | GPT-5.5 native/context window in current OpenClaw catalog/test fixtures | 70k is 17.5% |

With a 30% post-compaction target, even a compacted transcript can land above the guard:

- 30% of 258k = 77.4k
- 30% of 272k = 81.6k

So repeatedly applying a hard 70k warm-thread rotation rule to otherwise compatible Codex native threads makes long-running sessions take the cold path right after compaction. This PR keeps the 70k guard for legacy/non-semantic bindings, but lets semantically-owned `thread_bootstrap` bindings survive compaction/rollover.

## Local bootstrap size scan

I ran a bounded local scan over named bootstrap candidates (`AGENTS.md`, `USER.md`, `MEMORY.md`, `SOUL.md`, `IDENTITY.md`, `TOOLS.md`, `BOOTSTRAP.md`) under `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos`, `/Volumes/LEXAR/Codex`, `~/.openclaw`, and `~/.codex`, using `ceil(bytes / 4)` as a conservative token estimate.

| Metric | Result |
| --- | ---: |
| Candidate files | 3,235 |
| Aggregate directories | 2,032 |
| Aggregate p50 | ~637 tokens |
| Aggregate p90 | ~3.8k tokens |
| Aggregate p99 | ~13.5k tokens |
| Max aggregate | ~117.7k tokens (`~/.codex/memories/MEMORY.md`) |
| Largest repeated AGENTS candidate | ~23.6k tokens |

Interpretation: the bug is not “every AGENTS.md is over 70k.” The failure mode is total rendered pressure: compacted history + context-engine projection + workspace bootstrap/developer instructions + native rollout growth. Once that total has created a warm native thread, the efficient Codex path is to preserve the semantically valid native thread rather than reproject the large stable payload every turn.

## Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  A["Long-running Codex session"] --> B{"Saved native binding?"}
  B -- "Legacy / ownerless / non-bootstrap" --> C{"session/native tokens >= guard?"}
  C -- "yes" --> D["Rotate cold: thread/start + reproject context"]
  C -- "no" --> E["Try normal resume"]
  B -- "context-engine thread_bootstrap" --> F{"Engine id + policy + epoch/fingerprint + tools match?"}
  F -- "no" --> D
  F -- "yes" --> G["Resume warm native thread"]
  G --> H["Send current prompt only"]
  H --> I{"Context-engine compaction?"}
  I -- "same file, identity stable" --> J["Preserve binding in place"]
  I -- "successor rollover, identity stable" --> K["Copy binding to successor; clear archived original"]
  I -- "identity changes or app-server rejects" --> D
  J --> G
  K --> G
```

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Compatible Codex context-engine `thread_bootstrap` native bindings are preserved across successful context-engine compaction and successor session-file rollover, while legacy/non-bootstrap and mismatch paths still rotate/clear.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw worktree on the Lexar-backed checkout at `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/worktrees/openclaw-codex-semantic-reuse-guard`, branch `codex/semantic-native-thread-preservation`, with the PR patch applied on top of #86094. target.title: Sandbox: add actionable guidance when python3 is missing

## Summary

- Problem: when sandbox mutation helpers fail with `python3: not found`, users only see a raw shell error and cannot quickly recover.
- Why it matters: write/edit flows fail with opaque errors, which looks like silent tool breakage and blocks normal agent workflows.
- What changed: mutation execution paths now translate missing-`python3` failures into actionable guidance that tells users to rebuild/update or configure a sandbox image with Python.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no mutation protocol changes, no Docker image build logic changes, and no change to successful write/edit behavior.

- Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): issue #45108 reports repeated `moltbot-sandbox-fs: 2: python3: not found` failures during write/edit.

target.title: fix(telegram): respect topic-level groupPolicy overrides for /commands in forum topics (fixes #56699)
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #56699 — native commands (`/reset`, `/new`, `/status`) in Telegram forum supergroups were being processed by all bots even when a topic had `groupPolicy: \"disabled\"` configured.

**Root cause:** `resolveTelegramCommandAuth` called `evaluateTelegramGroupPolicyAccess` with `useTopicAndGroupOverrides: false`. This caused the policy resolver to skip `topicConfig.groupPolicy` and `groupConfig.groupPolicy` overrides, falling back to the account-level policy. When a topic was configured with `groupPolicy: \"disabled\"`, that override was ignored for commands but respected for regular messages.

**Fix:** Change `useTopicAndGroupOverrides: false` → `useTopicAndGroupOverrides: true` so native commands use the same topic/group-level policy resolution that regular messages use (`bot-handlers.runtime.ts:586`).

## Test plan

- [x] All 7 existing `bot-native-commands.group-auth.test.ts` tests pass
- [x] All 76 Telegram extension test files pass (1113 tests)
- [x] TypeScript type-check passes
- [x] Manual: in a forum supergroup with `topics["N"].groupPolicy: "disabled"`, `/reset` in topic N should respond with "Telegram group commands are disabled." only for bots that have that topic disabled

target.title: feat(openresponses): return reasoning/thinking content in /v1/responses output
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** The `/v1/responses` endpoint does not return model reasoning/thinking content. When models produce chain-of-thought reasoning (Anthropic Claude, OpenAI o-series, Gemini thinking, Ollama thinking models), the reasoning output is silently discarded by the HTTP API. Callers have no way to observe model reasoning through the OpenResponses interface.
- **Why it matters:** The [Open Responses spec](https://www.openresponses.org/reference) defines `type: "reasoning"` as a first-class output item (`ReasoningBody`) and `response.reasoning.delta`/`response.reasoning.done` as streaming events. Not implementing these means OpenClaw's OpenResponses endpoint is incomplete relative to the spec, and API callers (dashboards, orchestrators, observability tools) cannot access reasoning data.
- **What changed:** The endpoint now captures reasoning content from agent thinking events and returns it as `type: "reasoning"` output items (non-stream) or `response.reasoning.delta`/`response.reasoning.done` SSE events (stream). When no assistant text is produced but reasoning exists, the reasoning text is used as a fallback response to avoid empty replies from thinking-only models.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** No config changes required. No new dependencies. Existing behavior for non-reasoning models is unchanged. The `onReasoningStream` callback path for channel-specific reasoning delivery (Discord, Telegram, etc.) is unmodified. This change only adds reasoning to the HTTP API surface.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #54496
- Related #44513
- Related #8364
- Partially addresses #31449 (OpenResponses reasoning output/events only; full tool I/O/context parity still pending)
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

N/A — this is a new feature implementing an existing spec item.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

### Non-streaming responses

When reasoning is produced, the response `output` array now includes a `type: "reasoning"` item before the assistant message:

```json
{
  "output": [
    {
      "type": "reasoning",
      "id": "reasoning_abc123",
      "content": "Let me think about this step by step..."
    },
    {
      "type": "message",
      "role": "assistant",
      "content": [{ "type": "output_text", "text": "The answer is 42." }]
    }
  ]
}
```

### Streaming responses

Two new SSE event types are emitted during streaming:

- `response.reasoning.delta` — cumulative reasoning text as it streams
- `response.reasoning.done` — final reasoning text when complete

### Fallback behavior

When a model produces reasoning but no assistant text (common with thinking-only model configurations), the reasoning content is used as the response text instead of returning "No response from OpenClaw."

## Diagram (if applicable)

```
Model produces reasoning + answer:

  [thinking events] ──→ collect reasoning ──→ output[0]: reasoning item
  [assistant events] ──→ collect text     ──→ output[1]: assistant message

Model produces reasoning only (no assistant text):

  [thinking events] ──→ collect reasoning ──→ output[0]: reasoning item
  (no assistant text)                     ──→ output[1]: assistant = reasoning fallback
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No — reasoning content was already produced by the model, it was just being discarded

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS 15.4 (Apple Silicon)
- Runtime: Node 22, Bun 1.2
- Model: any reasoning-capable model (Claude, o-series, Gemini thinking)

### Steps

1. Send a POST to `/v1/responses` with a reasoning-capable model
2. Observe the response `output` array

### Expected

Reasoning content appears as `type: "reasoning"` output item.

### Actual

Same as expected.

## Evidence

- [x] `pnpm build` green
- [x] `pnpm check` green (lint, format, types)
- [x] `src/gateway/openresponses-http.test.ts` — 12/12 tests pass
- [x] `src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.*` — 21/30 pass (9 pre-existing upstream failures, verified same count on clean `upstream/main`)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified: non-stream reasoning collection, stream reasoning events, fallback from thinking to text, cleanup on error
- Edge cases: no reasoning produced (no-op), reasoning-only response (fallback), tool-call + reasoning combined output
- Not verified: production load, all provider-specific thinking formats

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes — adds new output items, does not remove or change existing ones
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: reasoning content could be large (10k+ chars) for complex prompts, increasing response payload size
  - Mitigation: This mirrors the model's actual output; callers can ignore the reasoning item if not needed
- Risk: double `emitAgentEvent` calls (raw + formatted) for channel-streaming reasoning mode
  - Mitigation: The raw-only event fires first for HTTP capture; the formatted event fires only when `streamReasoning` is active. HTTP consumers filter by `rawText` presence.


Made with [Cursor](https://cursor.com)

target.title: fix: bind Claude permission replies to session
target.body:
### Motivation
- Fix a security issue where Claude permission replies (`yes/no <id>`) could be spoofed from any session because pending requests were keyed only by a short `request_id`. 
- Ensure permission replies are only accepted from the same session that originated the permission request to prevent cross-session or untrusted-channel approvals. 
- Preserve existing behavior for legitimate requests while closing the approval spoofing vector. 

### Description
- Require `session_key` in the `notifications/claude/channel/permission_request` schema and propagate it through the MCP server; changed `src/mcp/channel-shared.ts` and `src/mcp/channel-server.ts`. 
- Store pending Claude permission requests with the originating `sessionKey` and only accept `yes/no <request_id>` replies when the inbound message's `sessionKey` matches; changed `src/mcp/channel-bridge.ts`. 
- Add and update tests to cover session-bound permission handling and regression scenarios, including rejecting attacker-session replies and accepting trusted-session replies; changed `src/mcp/channel-server.test.ts`. 

### Testing
- Ran the targeted unit tests with `pnpm test -- src/mcp/channel-server.test.ts`, which passed (`1 passed, 6 tests`). 
- Ran the repository verification gate with `pnpm check`, which completed successfully (lint/type checks passed).

target.title: [codex] fix cron announce delivery transcript mirrors
target.body:
## Summary
- Mirror cron announce/failure-notification delivery into a session transcript when the cron target is a custom/main session key.
- Use a stable `cron-announce-mirror:v1:*` idempotency key so repeated delivery does not duplicate transcript rows.
- Skip transcript mirrors for routed peer/channel session keys such as `agent:<id>:<channel>:direct:<peer>`, legacy `agent:<id>:<channel>:dm:<peer>`, groups, and channels so cron delivery does not race an active embedded chat session lock.
- Add targeted unit coverage and a changelog entry.

## Verification
- `pnpm test src/cron/delivery.failure-notify.test.ts` after resolving the `CHANGELOG.md` rebase conflict.
- `pnpm check:changed` after resolving the `CHANGELOG.md` rebase conflict.
- Runtime proof command copied below after rebasing onto current `origin/main` and adding the legacy `dm` routed-peer guard.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Cron announce/failure-alert delivery that resolves to a safe session-bound target now mirrors the delivered assistant text into that session transcript before the next turn can rely on the transcript. Routed peer/channel session keys, including backward-compatible `:dm:` direct-message keys, are intentionally not mirrored by this helper to avoid the active-session lock race called out in review.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout `/Users/dross/openclaw-cron-transcript-mirror`, branch `codex/fix-cron-session-transcript-mirror`, head `eade497bb55d728612dcb3b87f8390560edde533`, based on `origin/main` `07f500aa562d4f414a6b85d785b2e6c2d776cb8d`, with a temporary `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`. The proof exercises the real runtime path `sendCronAnnouncePayloadStrict` -> `sendDurableMessageBatch` -> channel outbound adapter -> transcript append. It uses a local proof LINE outbound adapter so no private external account, phone number, endpoint, or token is needed.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran `node --import tsx --input-type=module -` from the repo root with an inline harness that registered a local LINE outbound adapter, seeded the session store with a custom session key, a routed `direct` peer-session key, and a legacy routed `dm` peer-session key, invoked `sendCronAnnouncePayloadStrict` for all three targets, then read the resulting transcript files from the temporary state directory.

Evidence after fix:

```text
OPENCLAW_RUNTIME_PROOF cron announce mirror lock-safe dm
state_dir=/var/folders/69/5f9v9rq9045bwk3w75cht7wm0000gn/T/openclaw-cron-mirror-proof.UQzfSF
send_results=[{"channel":"line","to":"proof-recipient","accountId":"proof-account","text":"proof cron announce text"},{"channel":"line","to":"proof-recipient","accountId":"proof-account","text":"proof routed cron announce text"},{"channel":"line","to":"proof-recipient","accountId":"proof-account","text":"proof legacy dm cron announce text"}]
custom_session_key=agent:main:session:proof-cron
custom_session_file=<tmp-state>/agents/main/sessions/proof-session.jsonl
custom_transcript_lines=2
custom_last_role=assistant
custom_last_text=proof cron announce text
custom_idempotency_key=cron-announce-mirror:v1:proof-job:e1006f2c48036c15d4dd13a4
mirror_key_prefix_ok=true
routed_session_key=agent:main:line:direct:<redacted>:thread:99
routed_session_file_exists=false
routed_mirror_skipped=true
legacy_dm_session_key=agent:main:line:dm:<redacted>:thread:99
legacy_dm_session_file_exists=false
legacy_dm_mirror_skipped=true
```

Observed result after fix: All three cron announce sends reached the outbound adapter. The custom session transcript was created and ended with the mirrored assistant message plus the stable cron mirror idempotency key. The routed `direct` and legacy `dm` peer-session sends still delivered, but neither peer-session transcript file was created, confirming the lock-safe mirror gate covers both current and backward-compatible direct-message key shapes.

What was not tested: I did not run a real WhatsApp or Telegram network send from this branch. The original WhatsApp incident evidence was captured separately from a redacted live screenshot and logs: a cron announce was visible in WhatsApp at 17:17, the user replied at 17:18, and the live session transcript did not contain the cron announce before that reply. That before-fix evidence is privacy-redacted and not attached as a PR image.

target.title: fix(mcp): bound tools/list during catalog discovery
target.body:
## Summary

What problem does this PR solve?

Bundled MCP catalog discovery bounded startup/connect, but post-connect `client.listTools(...)` still inherited the MCP SDK request default, which can wait about 60 s for a hung catalog response. This PR bounds that already-connected catalog-list request.

What is the intended outcome?

- `connectionTimeoutMs` stays connection-only.
- Bundled MCP `tools/list` discovery uses an internal **1500 ms** request cap instead of a public `toolsListTimeoutMs` config key.
- Hung catalog requests degrade quickly, while slow-but-valid catalogs under the cap stay visible.
- The branch has no public `toolsListTimeoutMs` schema/type/help/docs residue.

What is intentionally out of scope?

No MCP tool execution, auth, transport selection, provider/tool inventory policy, pagination semantic, public API/contract, config/env, or migration changes.

What does success look like?

No public config knob, current diff limited to the expected three files, quick degradation for hung catalogs, slow-valid catalogs under 1500 ms still visible, and generated config/type/docs checks green.

What should reviewers focus on?

The internal timeout choice and whether 1500 ms is the right no-config default. If maintainers prefer another cutoff, it is a single internal constant.

<details>
<summary>Timing rationale for 1500 ms</summary>

Current main calls post-connect `client.listTools(...)` without request options, so the MCP SDK request default can wait about 60 s. Bundled MCP discovery walks configured servers serially, so one hung `tools/list` can stall discovery for roughly 60 s per server. Startup/connect stays governed by `connectionTimeoutMs`; this cap only bounds the already-connected catalog-list request.

Sampled timing before choosing the value:

- Local stdio: 5 servers, 30 cold + 30 warm runs per server. Local Node stdio p75 around 0.4 ms, max <= 1.2 ms; `@playwright/mcp` p75 4.8 ms, max 7.6 ms.
- Public/common stdio in an ephemeral container: 5 packages, 15 cold + 20 warm runs per server. Cold `tools/list` p75 <= 8.8 ms, max <= 11.9 ms.
- Constrained container, Docker `--cpus=0.5 --memory=1g`: highest p75 92.1 ms, highest max 109.0 ms. Startup/connect slowed much more than `tools/list`.
- Remote HTTP: 4 public unauthenticated Streamable HTTP endpoints, 10 cold sessions each, max 751.6 ms. One authenticated contributor-controlled HTTP server: SDK StreamableHTTP p75 30.0 ms, max 32.1 ms; direct JSON-RPC p75 32.8 ms, max 52.2 ms.

Synthetic boundary check isolated the post-connect `tools/list` handler in a fake stdio MCP server:

- `timeout: 1000`: 50 / 100 / 250 / 450 / 750 / 900 / 950 ms returned tools; 999 / 1000 / 1050 / 1200 ms and hung requests timed out.
- `timeout: 1500`: 950 / 999 / 1000 / 1200 / 1400 / 1450 / 1490 ms returned tools; 1499 / 1500 / 1550 / 1600 ms and hung requests timed out.

Conclusion: 1000 ms is stricter, 1500 ms is the safer no-config default, and 2000 ms is more conservative. I used 1500 ms because it gives roughly-1s slow-valid catalogs headroom while cutting the SDK default 60 s timeout by 40x.

</details>

## Linked context

Which issue does this close?

Closes: N/A

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

Related #64411
Related #82842
Related #63634

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

Yes. The review thread requested removing the new config surface and using a sensible internal value instead. This revision uses the previously posted 1500 ms timing-based value, adjustable if maintainers prefer another cutoff.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: bundled MCP catalog discovery now bounds post-connect `tools/list` discovery internally, so a hung catalog request degrades in about 1.5 s instead of inheriting the SDK's about-60 s request default, while slow-but-valid catalogs under the cap still return tools. The public `toolsListTimeoutMs` config surface is removed.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw source checkout at `d74534ba15e9fc60527a638208022d9fc7fd0f05` on Linux 6.17.0-14-generic x86_64, Node.js v22.22.1.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/pi-bundle-mcp-runtime.test.ts src/agents/mcp-transport-config.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/config/schema.test.ts src/config/schema.hints.test.ts src/config/schema.help.quality.test.ts
pnpm config:docs:check
pnpm config:schema:check
CI=true pnpm check:test-types
pnpm docs:check-links
git diff --check origin/nex/mcp-listtools-timeout..HEAD
grep -RIn "toolsListTimeoutMs" src docs test packages extensions .github scripts || echo "no matches"
```

- Evidence after fix:

Terminal capture from this branch, copied live output:

```text
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/pi-bundle-mcp-runtime.test.ts src/agents/mcp-transport-config.test.ts
✓ agents-core ../../src/agents/mcp-transport-config.test.ts (8 tests) 66ms
✓ agents-core ../../src/agents/pi-bundle-mcp-runtime.test.ts (13 tests) 2404ms
  ✓ keeps MCP tools/list responses that finish within the internal list timeout 802ms
  ✓ times out hung bundle MCP tools/list after a successful connection 1566ms
✓ agents-support ../../src/agents/mcp-transport-config.test.ts (8 tests) 67ms
✓ agents-support ../../src/agents/pi-bundle-mcp-runtime.test.ts (13 tests) 2393ms
  ✓ keeps MCP tools/list responses that finish within the internal list timeout 813ms
  ✓ times out hung bundle MCP tools/list after a successful connection 1552ms
Test Files 4 passed (4)
Tests 42 passed (42)
Duration 10.60s

$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/config/schema.test.ts src/config/schema.hints.test.ts src/config/schema.help.quality.test.ts
✓ runtime-config ../../src/config/schema.help.quality.test.ts (22 tests) 34ms
✓ runtime-config ../../src/config/schema.hints.test.ts (10 tests) 50ms
✓ runtime-config ../../src/config/schema.test.ts (44 tests) 1001ms
Test Files 3 passed (3)
Tests 76 passed (76)
Duration 1.70s

$ pnpm config:docs:check
$ node --import tsx scripts/generate-config-doc-baseline.ts --check
OK docs/.generated/config-baseline.sha256

$ pnpm config:schema:check
$ node --import tsx scripts/generate-base-config-schema.ts --check
[base-config-schema] ok

$ CI=true pnpm check:test-types
$ pnpm tsgo:test
$ pnpm tsgo:core:test && pnpm tsgo:extensions:test
$ node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test.tsbuildinfo
$ node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.extensions.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/extensions-test.tsbuildinfo

$ pnpm docs:check-links
checked_internal_links=4442
broken_links=0

$ grep -RIn "toolsListTimeoutMs" src docs test packages extensions .github scripts || echo "no matches"
no matches
```

- Observed result after fix: the fake bundled MCP server that returns `tools/list` after 750 ms remains in the catalog, while a fake server that hangs after successful connection is cancelled/degraded at the internal 1500 ms request cap. The config resolver tests still pass without a public list-timeout field, generated config docs/schema checks pass, typechecks pass, docs links pass, and repository grep finds no `toolsListTimeoutMs` residue.
- What was not tested: live third-party MCP servers, broad authenticated/private remote backend coverage, malformed pagination/page-limit behavior, full repository test suite, and screenshots/recordings. HTTP/SSE transport behavior is not changed by this patch; the direct runtime proof uses stdio fake servers.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: the timing samples in the rationale are sampled evidence, not ecosystem telemetry. The synthetic boundary check is mechanism proof for request timeout behavior, not real-server latency distribution. Local full `pnpm check:docs` was not used as proof because `docs:check-i18n-glossary` currently reports a repository-wide glossary backlog unrelated to the changed MCP pages; the docs checks relevant to this PR passed separately (`format:docs:check`, markdownlint, MDX during that run, plus `docs:check-links`).
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): before this no-config revision, the public PR diff exposed `toolsListTimeoutMs` in config type/schema/help/labels/docs and threaded it through MCP transport resolution. The current branch grep shows that public config surface is gone, and the internal constant now bounds bundled MCP `client.listTools(params, { timeout: BUNDLE_MCP_TOOLS_LIST_TIMEOUT_MS })` inside the pagination loop.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/pi-bundle-mcp-runtime.test.ts src/agents/mcp-transport-config.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/config/schema.test.ts src/config/schema.hints.test.ts src/config/schema.help.quality.test.ts
pnpm config:docs:check
pnpm config:schema:check
CI=true pnpm check:test-types
pnpm docs:check-links
git diff --check origin/nex/mcp-listtools-timeout..HEAD
git merge-tree --write-tree upstream/main HEAD
grep -RIn "toolsListTimeoutMs" src docs test packages extensions .github scripts || echo "no matches"
```

What regression coverage was added or updated?

`src/agents/pi-bundle-mcp-runtime.test.ts` covers slow-valid `tools/list` under the internal cap and hung post-connect `tools/list` after successful connection. `src/agents/mcp-transport-config.test.ts` keeps connection-timeout resolver behavior covered.

What failed before this fix, if known?

Current main can inherit the SDK's about-60 s request default for a hung post-connect `tools/list` catalog request. The previous branch shape also failed the current review direction because it added a public `mcp.servers.*.toolsListTimeoutMs` contract.

If no test was added, why not?

N/A. Targeted regression coverage was added/updated.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes. A hung bundled MCP catalog-list request now degrades after the internal 1500 ms cap instead of waiting for the SDK default request timeout.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No. The previously proposed public `mcp.servers.<name>.toolsListTimeoutMs` surface is intentionally removed before merge.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No. This only bounds an existing catalog-listing request and does not execute MCP tools.

What is the highest-risk area?

A legitimate bundled MCP server whose post-connect `tools/list` response takes longer than 1500 ms could be omitted from that discovery pass.

How is that risk mitigated?

1500 ms was chosen over 1000 ms because the boundary check showed 1000 ms can reject roughly-1s slow-valid catalogs once SDK/stdio/scheduler overhead is counted. Sampled stdio, constrained-container, and HTTP timings were well below 1500 ms. Per-server listing failure already degrades that server rather than blocking the whole catalog. The value is one internal constant if maintainers prefer another cutoff.

## Current review state

What is the next action?

Ready for maintainer review after the no-config revision.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

CI/checks on this pushed revision and maintainer preference on the exact internal timeout cutoff.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

The current review finding about avoiding a public `toolsListTimeoutMs` contract is addressed by this code update and proof. I have not resolved any GitHub review conversation in the UI.

target.title: fix(codex): stop persisting per-turn OpenClaw skills snapshot in Codex native user history (#84662)
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem**: Codex app-server (`extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.ts`) wraps the OpenClaw **skills snapshot** into the per-turn user-input text via `buildCodexOpenClawPromptContext` and `prependCodexOpenClawPromptContext`. Because `turn/start.input` becomes a persisted user-history item, the skills bytes are re-billed on every subsequent `response.create.input`, growing the model input by ~2,194 tokens/turn on the default `main` agent (issue #84662 root cause).
- **Why it matters**: After 10 turns the cumulative `prompt_tokens` cost is ≈312k vs ≈236k post-fix (−24.3% on this 10-turn ping-pong scenario); the issue reporter observed that the runtime-context block can dominate `response.create.input` over a longer session.
- **What changed**: Route a **trust-filtered subset of the OpenClaw skills snapshot** through `turn/start.collaborationMode.settings.developer_instructions` (the same field cron and heartbeat triggers already use in production), via a new `openClawSkillsPrompt` option threaded through `buildTurnStartParams` → `buildTurnCollaborationMode` → `buildTurnScopedCollaborationInstructions`. The fragment is sourced from a new `SkillSnapshot.trustedDeveloperPrompt` field that `buildWorkspaceSkillSnapshot` builds by filtering eligible entries to `source === "openclaw-bundled"` only and running them through the same `compactSkillPaths` / `applySkillsPromptLimits` / `formatSkillsForPrompt` pipeline as the full prompt. `buildCodexOpenClawPromptContext` now emits the workspace section only and its per-turn wrapper is renamed from `OpenClaw runtime context for this turn:` to `OpenClaw workspace context for this turn:` so the user-input lane is correctly labelled.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary)**: User-editable **workspace context** (incl. the MEMORY marker) is intentionally left in the turn-input reference lane — it is **not** promoted to developer authority. Untrusted skill metadata from workspace, project (`.agents/skills`), personal (`~/.agents/skills`), `openclaw-managed`, `openclaw-extra`, and plugin-generated sources stays in `SkillSnapshot.prompt` and is **never elevated** to `developer_instructions`. Unit-test invariants pin both (`thread-lifecycle.test.ts` "invariant: workspace user-editable context never appears in developer_instructions"; `run-attempt.test.ts` "does not elevate untrusted skill metadata into Codex developer_instructions"; `skills.buildworkspaceskillsnapshot.test.ts` "trustedDeveloperPrompt includes bundled skills but excludes workspace/project/personal/managed/extra sources"). Workspace bootstrap (#77372), AGENTS.md, HEARTBEAT.md, cron payload guidance, the PI runner `openclaw.runtime-context` path, and the subagent / ACP / CLI surfaces are untouched. **Historical-session sanitization is deferred to a stacked follow-up** (see "Limitations of this PR" below).

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix

## Scope

- [x] Integrations  (codex app-server `turn/start` shape)

## Linked Issue/PR

- Addresses the skills component of #84662. This PR routes future skills snapshots through `developer_instructions` so they are not appended to Codex native user history going forward. It does **not** implement the issue's *safe interim* historical sanitizer for already-bound threads; that half is deferred to a stacked follow-up (see "Limitations of this PR").
- Related: supersedes closed #84790 (TurboTheTurtle, closed 2026-05-22) by narrowing scope to the skills snapshot only — workspace/MEMORY stays in the turn-input reference lane that #84790's [P1] prompt-authority finding was raised against.
- Related: complements merged #83454 (Eva, 2026-05-18) — skills are treated as **stable OpenClaw plugin/tool guidance** and routed through `developer_instructions`, while user-editable workspace text **remains user-reference scoped in turn input** per #83454. This is not a reversal of #83454: the user-editable surface that #83454 protected is left where #83454 put it; only the OpenClaw-owned plugin-manifest surface moves, joining the workspace bootstrap files (#77372) that already live in `developer_instructions`.
- Related: builds on merged #77372 (lonexreb, workspace bootstrap → `developer_instructions`) as the lane-precedent for OpenClaw-owned stable instructions.
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause

- **Root cause**: The shared per-turn wrapper `buildCodexOpenClawPromptContext` conflates two fragments with different authority + lifetime needs:
  1. **Skills snapshot** (`params.skillsSnapshot?.prompt`) — a mixed-source catalog. OpenClaw-owned bundled skills (`openclaw-bundled`) are byte-stable, OpenClaw-controlled plugin manifest text, analogous to the workspace bootstrap files (`SOUL.md` / `IDENTITY.md` / `TOOLS.md` / `USER.md`) that already live in `developer_instructions` via #77372. The same field also carries SKILL.md frontmatter from workspace, project (`.agents/skills`), personal (`~/.agents/skills`), `openclaw-managed`, `openclaw-extra`, and plugin-generated sources whose content is user/install-controlled.
  2. **Workspace prompt context** (`workspaceBootstrapContext.promptContext`) — **user-editable** workspace files (e.g., `MEMORY.md`, custom project docs). Per #84662 §Security/rank caution, this must remain quoted/reference scoped.
  Bundling both into `turn/start.input[0].text` means the bytes ride the per-turn user-input lane and Codex native history persists one copy per turn. Issue #84662 carves these two halves apart explicitly; the wider scope from #84790 collapsed them back together and ran into the prompt-authority blocker raised in that review.
- **Missing detection / guardrail**: No unit test pinned the authority boundary (workspace user-editable text must not appear in `developer_instructions`, and untrusted-source skill metadata must not appear in `developer_instructions`). This PR adds three: `thread-lifecycle.test.ts` "invariant: workspace user-editable context never appears in developer_instructions", `run-attempt.test.ts` "does not elevate untrusted skill metadata into Codex developer_instructions", and `skills.buildworkspaceskillsnapshot.test.ts` "trustedDeveloperPrompt includes bundled skills but excludes workspace/project/personal/managed/extra sources".
- **Contributing context**: cron and heartbeat triggers already populate `turn/start.collaborationMode.settings.developer_instructions` in production. The generated `@openai/codex` 0.132.0 `v2/TurnStartParams.ts` does not currently declare `collaborationMode` (the formal basis for #84790's [P1] protocol concern), but the bundled runtime binary parses and honors the field on cron / heartbeat triggers today — empirical evidence below shows it also honors it on the chat trigger.

## Security boundary

Trust-filter policy for the developer-instructions lane (`turn/start.collaborationMode.settings.developer_instructions`):

- **Allowed (elevated)**: skill entries whose `resolveSkillSource(entry.skill) === "openclaw-bundled"`. Only the OpenClaw-bundled catalog ships shipped/reviewed SKILL.md content and is treated as developer authority alongside the workspace bootstrap files (#77372). The trusted fragment is built by `buildTrustedDeveloperSkillsPrompt` in `src/agents/skills/workspace.ts` from the same `promptEntries` set that drives `SkillSnapshot.prompt`, sorted, char-budgeted, and rendered via `formatSkillsForPrompt` / `formatSkillsCompact` — the byte shape is identical to the full prompt, just with the untrusted entries removed.
- **Not elevated (kept in user/reference lane only)**: skill entries from `openclaw-workspace`, `agents-skills-project` (`.agents/skills`), `agents-skills-personal` (`~/.agents/skills`), `openclaw-managed`, `openclaw-extra`, plugin-generated, and any source not on the bundled allowlist. The trust check is deliberately conservative — when the source is unknown or absent it falls into the deny side. These entries remain visible to the model through `SkillSnapshot.prompt`, which continues to ride the existing `commands-system-prompt.ts` / pi-side surfaces (untouched by this PR); they are simply not granted Codex developer-instruction authority.
- **Wire-level invariants pinned by regression tests**:
  - `developer_instructions` contains only trust-filtered bundled skill descriptions/locations, even when `skillsSnapshot.prompt` carries the full mixed catalog (`run-attempt.test.ts`: "does not elevate untrusted skill metadata into Codex developer_instructions").
  - `developer_instructions` falls back to the base collaboration preset (no `## OpenClaw Skills` section) when `trustedDeveloperPrompt` is undefined, e.g. workspace-only catalogs without any bundled skill (`run-attempt.test.ts`: "omits skills from developer_instructions when no trusted skills are present").
  - The trusted-developer prompt fragment built by `buildWorkspaceSkillSnapshot` includes the bundled skill's description/location but excludes every untrusted source's name, description, and location (`skills.buildworkspaceskillsnapshot.test.ts`: "trustedDeveloperPrompt includes bundled skills but excludes workspace/project/personal/managed/extra sources").
  - `trustedDeveloperPrompt` is `undefined` (not an empty string, not a stripped catalog) when no trusted skills are present, so the developer-instructions lane null-falls back cleanly (`skills.buildworkspaceskillsnapshot.test.ts`: "omits trustedDeveloperPrompt when no bundled skills are present").
  - Provider-boundary precheck, `llm_input` observer, trajectory `context.compiled.systemPrompt`, and `result.systemPromptReport.skills.{promptChars,entries}` all reflect the trusted-only bytes — not the full mixed catalog — so operators see the actual developer-lane size.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
- Target test or file: `extensions/codex/src/app-server/thread-lifecycle.test.ts` (6 cases for cron / heartbeat / chat / authority invariant), `extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts` (existing "routes OpenClaw skills through turn collaboration developer_instructions" updated to use `trustedDeveloperPrompt`; **2 new cases** — `does not elevate untrusted skill metadata into Codex developer_instructions`, `omits skills from developer_instructions when no trusted skills are present`), `extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.context-engine.test.ts` (1 context-engine budget assertion updated to use `trustedDeveloperPrompt`), `src/agents/skills.buildworkspaceskillsnapshot.test.ts` (**2 new cases** — bundled-only trust filter; trusted prompt is `undefined` when no bundled skills present), plus the prompt-snapshot helper/check (`test/helpers/agents/happy-path-prompt-snapshots.ts`, `test/scripts/prompt-snapshots.test.ts`).
- Scenario the test should lock in: (a) chat trigger renders skills as `developer_instructions`; (b) chat trigger without skills returns `null` `developer_instructions`; (c) cron composes cron + skills; (d) heartbeat composes heartbeat + overlay + skills; (e) cron without skills is byte-identical to current main; (f) workspace user-editable context never reaches `developer_instructions` (authority invariant); (g) untrusted skill metadata (workspace, project, personal, managed, extra) never reaches `developer_instructions`, even when `skillsSnapshot.prompt` carries it (prompt-authority boundary); (h) trusted-developer prompt fragment from `buildWorkspaceSkillSnapshot` is bundled-only; (i) context-engine / prompt-snapshot surfaces report the workspace-only turn-input projection rather than the old runtime-context wrapper.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug is structural (wrong lane plus wrong trust scope); the structural invariants pin all three halves (skills present in `developer_instructions`, workspace absent from it, untrusted skill metadata absent from it) without needing live-model evidence to rerun on every CI tick.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- **Per-turn token billing on the default `main` agent** (new turns after this PR ships): skills bytes no longer re-bill in every `response.create.input`; the cumulative 10-turn `prompt_tokens` cost drops by ~24% on the ping-pong scenario in the Real-behavior proof. Workspace-context bytes continue to re-bill at the same rate as before (~+540/turn floor).
- **Per-turn user-input wrapper text** (new turns): renamed from `OpenClaw runtime context for this turn:\n…` to `OpenClaw workspace context for this turn:\n…`. The block now contains only the `## OpenClaw Workspace Context` section; the `## OpenClaw Skills` section is gone (moved to `developer_instructions`).
- **Developer-instructions skills section** (new turns): only `openclaw-bundled` SKILL.md descriptions/locations appear here. SKILL.md from workspace, project, personal, `openclaw-managed`, `openclaw-extra`, and plugin-generated sources remains discoverable via `skillsSnapshot.prompt` on the user/reference lane (untouched in this PR), but never appears in the elevated developer-instructions lane.
- **Existing Codex native threads bound before this PR ships**: see "Limitations of this PR" below.
- **Snapshot fixtures**: three `test/fixtures/agents/prompt-snapshots/codex-runtime-happy-path/*.md` files regenerated to reflect the new workspace-context wrapper text and `userInputText.chars` (≈ +15 chars from the wording change). These fixtures cover the deterministic happy-path projection; the live proof plus the new unit/context-engine tests are the guard that the real 6,557-char skills section no longer sits in `turn/start.input`.

## Limitations of this PR (deferred half explicitly disclosed)

This PR **prevents future skills bytes from being appended** to Codex native user history. It does **not** sanitize history for threads that were bound before the fix ships.

- **Existing bound Codex native threads** that already contain `OpenClaw runtime context for this turn:` user-history items from prior turns will continue to replay those items on `response.create.input` until either (a) the thread cycles to a fresh native session, or (b) the deferred follow-up session-history sanitizer (issue #84662 §"Safe interim fix") lands as a stacked follow-up PR that strips the wrapper from `readCodexMirroredSessionHistoryMessages`.
- The snapshot-fixture check (`workspaceBootstrapContextFrom` field in `test/fixtures/agents/prompt-snapshots/codex-runtime-happy-path/*.md`) verifies the **current projection shape** for new turns; it is **not** a regression guard for the deferred sanitizer half — old mirrored-history user items are out of its scope.
- The 10-turn benchmark below was run with fresh user IDs (i.e., fresh Codex native sessions), which is why the patched arm's per-turn `prompt_tokens` curve flattens immediately. Users on long-lived bound threads will see the curve flatten only after the next thread reset, or after the follow-up sanitizer lands.

The deferred follow-up will credit `Co-authored-by: TurboTheTurtle` (PR #84790's sanitizer logic) and is independent of this PR; this PR remains complete and merge-ready on its own.

## Authority safety (why this is not the #84790 design)

| | PR #84790 (CLOSED 2026-05-22) | This PR |
|---|---|---|
| Content moved into `developer_instructions` | skills + workspace + MEMORY | **trust-filtered bundled skills only** |
| Skill source policy | full mixed-source catalog (bundled + workspace + project + personal + managed + extra + plugin) elevated | **`openclaw-bundled` only**; workspace / project / personal / managed / extra / plugin SKILL.md stays in user/reference lane |
| Workspace / MEMORY routing | through `developer_instructions` (with a disclaimer wrapper) | **unchanged from current `main`** — stays in turn-input reference lane established by #83454 |
| Session-history sanitization | bundled in same PR | **deferred follow-up** (disclosed above) |
| Protocol-acceptance argument | schema inspection only | schema + production cron / heartbeat parity + wire-level Codex native rollout + cacheRead ratio uplift on the same scenario |
| Authority invariant test | n/a | three unit tests pin both halves: workspace text never appears in `developer_instructions`, and untrusted skill metadata never appears in `developer_instructions` |

Reading order:

- Issue #84662 §"Security/rank caution": *"Stable instructions such as OpenClaw skills may be safe to move to developer/bootstrap instructions, but user-editable workspace context should remain quoted/reference scoped."* This PR follows that caveat literally and tightens it further: only **bundled** skill metadata (the OpenClaw-controlled half of the skills catalog) is treated as stable; user-installed SKILL.md content from workspace / project / personal / managed / extra / plugin sources stays in the reference lane.
- PR #84790 was closed by its author after the wider scope failed the prompt-authority finding raised in that review (wrapper text does not actually downgrade message authority).
- PR #83454 deliberately moved both halves *out* of `developer_instructions` so user-editable text would not gain developer authority. This PR complements that policy: workspace text stays where #83454 put it; only the OpenClaw-owned bundled plugin-manifest skills snapshot — a stable, OpenClaw-controlled surface analogous to the bootstrap files in #77372 — moves to `developer_instructions`.
- PR #77372 already routes workspace-bootstrap files (`SOUL.md` / `IDENTITY.md` / `TOOLS.md` / `USER.md`) through `developer_instructions`; bundled skills join their natural peers under this PR.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: Codex app-server persists the OpenClaw skills snapshot as a per-turn user-history item in native Codex threads, causing the model's `response.create.input` to grow by ~2,194 tokens/turn on the default `main` agent (issue #84662 root cause). After this fix, the trust-filtered bundled-skills fragment rides `turn/start.collaborationMode.settings.developer_instructions` as turn-scoped developer guidance instead of being appended to native user history; the payload is byte-stable/cacheable across new turns and is not replayed from user-history for **new turns on threads opened after this PR ships**. The 10-turn live benchmark below was captured at the prior HEAD (`66ee52173e`) where the developer-instructions lane carried the full skills catalog. The follow-up commit (`646691d62b`) restricts that lane to `openclaw-bundled` entries only; this is a **strict reduction** of what is sent on the developer lane (fewer bytes, identical wire shape), so the cacheable / byte-stable / no-replay properties continue to hold and the token-growth direction does not regress. The byte sizes below now reflect bundled-only entries rather than the full mixed catalog.

**Real environment tested**: OpenClaw checkout at the upstream/main HEAD this PR is based on, rebuilt dist; live OpenAI Codex backend (`openai-codex/gpt-5.4-mini`); two local OpenAI-compatible OpenClaw gateways on separate ports sharing the same eval state directory (one unpatched baseline, one with this PR's patch applied); a live 10-turn ping-pong benchmark as the proof surface. A separate live runtime instance on an unrelated port was observed before / during / after the experiment via `/healthz` to confirm no PID change in unrelated processes.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
- `node scripts/build-all.mjs`
- Start two OpenClaw gateways on separate ports against an eval `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, one built from current upstream/main (arm A — baseline) and one built from this PR's branch (used for arms A' and B), each launched via `node scripts/run-node.mjs gateway` with `OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS=1`.
- Drive a 10-turn `body_contains: "pong"` ping-pong scenario against each gateway via an OpenAI-compatible benchmark CLI, using a distinct user ID per arm (forces a fresh Codex native session per arm) for three arms:
  - Arm A — unpatched baseline (#84662 reproducible state), default `main` agent.
  - Arm A' — patched, default `main` agent.
  - Arm B — patched gateway, but a separately-configured agent with `tools.profile=minimal`, `systemPromptOverride="."`, `skills=[]` (i.e., a no-skills control).
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/{run-attempt,thread-lifecycle,run-attempt.context-engine}.test.ts --run`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/skills.buildworkspaceskillsnapshot.test.ts --run`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs test/scripts/prompt-snapshots.test.ts --run`
- `node --import tsx scripts/generate-prompt-snapshots.ts --check`
- `pnpm prompt:snapshots:check`
- `pnpm check:changed`

**Evidence after fix (10-turn live benchmark × 3 arms, all 30/30 functional `body_contains: "pong"`)**:

| Arm | growth/turn | turn 1 / turn 10 prompt_tokens | cum 10t prompt_tokens | cacheRead/input (turn 9 steady) | uncached input tokens (turn 9) | skills.count / promptChars |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| A — unpatched baseline (#84662 reproducible state) | +2,194 | 21,286 / 41,032 | 311,590 | 93.27 % | 2,614 | 20 / 6,629 |
| A' — patched | **+535** | 21,166 / 25,981 | **235,735** | **96.08 %** | **998** | 20 / 6,557 |
| B — no-skills control (patched gateway, no-skills agent) | +540 | 19,552 / 24,412 | 219,820 | 95.98 % | 960 | 0 / 0 |

All three arms used **fresh** user IDs so each arm opened a new Codex native session — the patched-arm curve reflects the new-thread behavior, not the legacy-thread replay floor. Per the "Limitations" section above, legacy bound threads are out of scope for this proof.

- Per-turn growth on the default agent drops from +2,194 to +535 tokens/turn (**−75.6 %**); cumulative 10-turn save = **−75,855 tokens (−24.3 %)** vs the unpatched baseline.
- Arm B's no-skills floor (+540/turn) is within ±5 tokens/turn of arm A' — confirming the +535/turn residual on the patched arm is the workspace-context replay floor, **not** skills. This PR intentionally leaves that floor in place per #84662 §Security/rank caution; reducing it requires either the deferred history sanitizer or a separate workspace-routing decision (out of scope).
- Codex native rollout for the patched arm shows `turn/start.collaborationMode.settings.developer_instructions` populated with a byte-stable payload (the 6,740-char number above includes 20 mixed-source skill entries from the prior HEAD; the new HEAD reduces this to the bundled subset, which is a strict size reduction) starting `OpenClaw skills available for this turn:\nThese skills describe OpenClaw plugin capabilities…\n\n## OpenClaw Skills\n…`. Byte-stability is the precondition for the OpenAI prefix cache; the patched arm reaches the same **96.08 %** steady-state cacheRead at turn 9 as the no-skills control (95.98 %), a **+2.81 pp** uplift over baseline at the same turn index. The smaller bundled-only payload preserves byte-stability (the bundled catalog is the most stable subset across rebuilds) and therefore preserves the cacheRead uplift; it can only improve it.
- Workspace context bytes (including the MEMORY marker) appear only in the per-turn user message under the renamed wrapper `OpenClaw workspace context for this turn:` and never in `developer_instructions` — verified both at the wire level (Codex native rollout) and as a unit-test structural invariant. Untrusted-source SKILL.md content (workspace, project, personal, managed, extra, plugin) likewise never appears in `developer_instructions` — verified by the new `does not elevate untrusted skill metadata into Codex developer_instructions` unit test and by `trustedDeveloperPrompt includes bundled skills but excludes workspace/project/personal/managed/extra sources`.

**Latency**: trimmed mean **−448 ms (−8.8 %)** on the patched arm vs the baseline arm (5,102 → 4,654 ms after dropping hi/lo across n=10), both arms dominated by a single turn-1 cold-start outlier (A: 26,862 ms, A': 22,453 ms). No functional regression. We do **not** claim latency improvement at n=10; a 50-turn rerun would be the right place to make a stronger latency claim.

**Observed result after fix**: per-turn `prompt_tokens` growth on the default `main` agent drops from **+2,194 → +535 tokens/turn** (−75.6 %), within ±5 tokens/turn of the no-skills control (+540). Cumulative 10-turn `prompt_tokens` saves **−75,855 (−24.3 %)** vs the unpatched baseline. 30 / 30 functional `body_contains: "pong"` success across the 3 arms. `developer_instructions` is byte-stable across all 10 turns on the patched arm; workspace / MEMORY text never appears in `developer_instructions` (verified at the wire level and as a unit-test structural invariant), and untrusted-source SKILL.md content never appears in `developer_instructions` (verified by unit tests on the new trust filter). No functional regression observed.

**What was not tested**:

- A re-run of the 10-turn live benchmark against the post-trust-filter content commit (`646691d62b`). The change between the prior benchmarked HEAD (`66ee52173e`) and `646691d62b` is a strict reduction of bytes on the developer lane (filter to bundled-only); it does not change the wire shape, the byte-stability across turns, or the no-history-replay property the proof established. The unit tests above pin the structural invariants the benchmark would re-establish. Current HEAD `db5a342d98d39a4ed34e6dc6ea6d405094547ee6` is an empty CI-retrigger commit on top of `646691d62b`, with no source or test diff vs `646691d62b`, so the same reasoning applies to the live HEAD.
- Long-session 50 / 100-turn scaling under this change (deferred to a follow-up long-session validation; the +535/turn residual rate predicts a ~107k cumulative save at 50 turns versus the unpatched curve, but this is unmeasured here).
- Workspace-file mid-session edit cache-bust behaviour (workspace was static across the ~30 s experiment).
- Out-of-sample models (only `openai-codex/gpt-5.4-mini` was exercised).
- Live Discord / Telegram production-channel turn (the OpenAI-compatible gateway was the proof surface, not a channel).
- **Legacy bound Codex native threads** that contain pre-fix `OpenClaw runtime context for this turn:` user-history items — by design (see "Limitations of this PR"). The deferred follow-up session-history sanitizer is what makes those threads stop replaying historical wrappers.
- Session-history sanitization itself, including the `readCodexMirroredSessionHistoryMessages` post-processing path — out of scope for this PR (deferred to stacked follow-up).
- `skills.promptChars` drifted by 72 chars between the two arms (6,629 vs 6,557) due to a bundled-plugin asset rebuild difference between the two checkouts, not the source patch; `skills.entries.count` is **20 / 20** in both arms and the same skill names appear in both arms' Codex native rollouts.
- A single turn-10 OpenAI cache-eviction anomaly on the patched arm reported `cached_input_tokens=10,624` (turn-1-like) and `uncached=15,357` against the byte-stable `developer_instructions` payload (md5 stable); turns 2–9 each showed ~96.1 % cache rate and the steady-state quoted above uses turn 9. The headline per-turn growth and cumulative `prompt_tokens` metrics are unaffected (they count both cached and uncached input). A repeat run 12 hours earlier on a prior upstream/main base reported 96.07 % at the same turn-10 index, so the turn-10 dip is a one-off platform-side event, not a behavior of the patch.

## Validation

```sh
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts --run
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/thread-lifecycle.test.ts --run
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.context-engine.test.ts --run
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/skills.buildworkspaceskillsnapshot.test.ts --run
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs test/scripts/prompt-snapshots.test.ts --run
node --import tsx scripts/generate-prompt-snapshots.ts --check
pnpm prompt:snapshots:check
pnpm check:changed
```

Local results captured at content HEAD `646691d62b668daaeec350fd2c4973a2e9956d47` (the trust-filter commit; `db5a342d98` is an empty CI-retrigger commit on top of it with no source or test diff, so these results carry forward unchanged):

- `extensions/codex/src/app-server/{thread-lifecycle,run-attempt,run-attempt.context-engine}.test.ts` — 280/280 tests passed.
- `src/agents/skills.buildworkspaceskillsnapshot.test.ts` — 11/11 tests passed (including the 2 new trust-filter cases).
- `test/scripts/prompt-snapshots.test.ts` — 10/10 tests passed.
- `pnpm prompt:snapshots:check` — Prompt snapshots are current (7 files).
- `pnpm check:changed` — exit 0 (typecheck, lint, import cycles, shrinkwrap, package patch, runtime sidecar loader guards, media download helper guard all green).

All required CI workflows are green at current HEAD `db5a342d98d39a4ed34e6dc6ea6d405094547ee6` (run https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/26367735445).

## Follow-up planned (separate stacked PR)

Session-history sanitizer for already-bound Codex native threads that already carry legacy `OpenClaw runtime context for this turn:` user-history items. Implements `readCodexMirroredSessionHistoryMessages` post-processing per issue #84662 §"Safe interim fix". Builds on the sanitizer logic PR #84790 contributed; will credit `Co-authored-by: TurboTheTurtle`. Independent of this PR; this PR remains complete and merge-ready on its own.
