target.title: fix: add inbound peer send policy matching
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds native `session.sendPolicy.rules[].match.peerEquals: "inboundPeer"` support.
- Allows relational send-policy checks between the outbound target and trusted inbound peer candidates for the current automatic-delivery turn, including `identityLinks` aliases.
- Threads inbound peer context through automatic replies, routed replies, follow-up routed replies, durable turn replies, and durable outbound delivery.
- Keeps send-policy-only predicates out of shared allow/deny scopes.
- Preserves explicit-send compatibility by keeping explicit `message.send`, gateway sends, and message-tool sends outside `session.sendPolicy` suppression.
- Persists `sendPolicyMode` for queued delivery replay and treats legacy queued entries without that field as explicit replay, preserving pre-PR queue semantics.
- Reports blocked automatic durable sends as `denied_by_send_policy` suppression outcomes.
- Adds focused unit coverage and docs/help labels for the new predicate.

Fixes #85175.

## Verification

Latest head: `86f2586aedc1966c00c99777135998dee47ba558`

Focused recovery, deliver, and send-policy validation requested by ClawSweeper:

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.infra.config.ts src/infra/outbound/delivery-queue.recovery.test.ts src/infra/outbound/deliver.test.ts
```

```text
node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/agents/tools/message-tool.test.ts src/infra/outbound/message-action-runner.plugin-dispatch.test.ts src/infra/outbound/message.test.ts src/infra/outbound/outbound-send-service.test.ts src/channels/message/send.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/send.test.ts src/channels/turn/durable-delivery.test.ts src/channels/turn/kernel.test.ts
```

Result:

```text
Gateway shard: 51 passed
Infra shard: 52 passed

```

Type/lint checks:

```text
pnpm tsgo:core
pnpm exec oxlint src/infra/outbound/delivery-queue-recovery.ts src/infra/outbound/delivery-queue.recovery.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/followup-runner.ts src/auto-reply/reply/skill-tool-dispatch.runtime.ts src/agents/openclaw-tools.plugin-context.ts src/agents/openclaw-tools.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/params.ts src/agents/pi-tools.ts src/agents/tools/message-tool.ts src/agents/tools/message-tool.test.ts src/infra/outbound/message-action-runner.ts src/infra/outbound/message-action-runner.plugin-dispatch.test.ts src/infra/outbound/message.ts src/infra/outbound/message.test.ts src/infra/outbound/outbound-send-service.ts src/infra/outbound/outbound-send-service.test.ts src/plugins/tool-types.ts
```

Result:

```text

oxlint passed
```

## Footprint reduction

The PR surface was reduced from ClawSweeper's reviewed `43 files / +1185 -58` to:

```text
30 files changed, 1038 insertions(+), 58 deletions(-)
```

Removed from the PR footprint:

- explicit message-tool `inboundPeer` propagation
- plugin tool-context `inboundPeer` propagation
- message-action-runner explicit-send `inboundPeer` propagation
- outbound-send-service explicit-send `inboundPeer` propagation
- related tests whose only purpose was proving explicit paths carried `inboundPeer`

Reasoning: explicit sends intentionally bypass `session.sendPolicy`, so passing `inboundPeer` through those explicit paths was unnecessary surface area. The automatic/durable reply path remains covered.

## Legacy queue compatibility

Recovery now treats queued delivery entries without `sendPolicyMode` as explicit replay:

```text
sendPolicyMode: entry.sendPolicyMode ?? "explicit"
```

This preserves pre-existing queued `message.send` / gateway retry behavior after upgrade under `session.sendPolicy.default: "deny"`.

Regression added:

```text
src/infra/outbound/delivery-queue.recovery.test.ts
- replays legacy queued entries without sendPolicyMode as explicit under deny-by-default policy
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** `session.sendPolicy.rules[].match.peerEquals: "inboundPeer"` must allow same-peer automatic Telegram durable replies and suppress wrong-peer automatic durable replies before platform send. The same PR also preserves explicit-send compatibility for legacy queued entries without `sendPolicyMode`.

- **Real environment tested:** Live Telegram bot setup using OpenClaw PR-head source at proof time, with a real Telegram direct peer `TG_PEER_A` and a distinct redacted target `TG_PEER_B`. Latest live Telegram proof comment: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/85763#issuecomment-4529388676. Current code-review head is `86f2586aedc1966c00c99777135998dee47ba558`; the live proof head was `e7b028ba5aa2c7444937deb020e838e130945f70`.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** On current head `86f2586aedc1966c00c99777135998dee47ba558`, I reran the focused recovery, deliver, send-policy, durable-reply, gateway-send, message-tool, typecheck, and lint commands listed in the Verification section. The live Telegram proof flow used the same durable `sendDurableMessageBatch` policy path: configure `session.sendPolicy` to deny direct Telegram when `{ "peerEquals": "inboundPeer", "invert": true }`, send one automatic durable reply to `TG_PEER_A`, then attempt an automatic durable send to `TG_PEER_B` in the same inbound-peer context.

- **Evidence after fix:** Redacted live Telegram proof comment: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/85763#issuecomment-4529388676. Copied redacted runtime output from that proof:

```text
Inbound Telegram direct session: agent:main:telegram:direct:TG_PEER_A_NUMERIC
Inbound peer candidates: TG_PEER_A, TG_PEER_A_NUMERIC, TG_PEER_A_USERNAME

Same-peer allow:
  outbound target: TG_PEER_A
  durable result: sent
  Telegram message id: 6
  Telegram API calls for this case: 1

Wrong-peer suppression:
  attempted outbound target: TG_PEER_B
  durable result: suppressed
  suppression reason: denied_by_send_policy
  cancel reason: send_policy_peer_mismatch
  Telegram API calls for this case: 0
```

Redacted diagnostic excerpt:

```json
{
  "status": "suppressed",
  "reason": "denied_by_send_policy",
  "hookEffect": {
    "cancelReason": "send_policy_peer_mismatch",
    "metadata": {
      "code": "send_policy_peer_mismatch",
      "peerEquals": "inboundPeer",
      "expectedPeers": ["TG_PEER_A", "TG_PEER_A_NUMERIC", "TG_PEER_A_USERNAME"],
      "actualPeer": "TG_PEER_B"
    }
  },
  "telegramApiCallsForCase": 0
}
```

Required proof gate status on current PR body/head:

```text
$ gh pr checks 85763 --repo openclaw/openclaw
Real behavior proof  pass  15s  https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/26369003419/job/77617646289

$ gh run view 26369003419 --repo openclaw/openclaw --log
External PR includes after-fix real behavior proof.
```

- **Observed result after fix:** Same-peer Telegram durable send to `TG_PEER_A` delivered with one Telegram API send call. Wrong-peer durable outbound attempt to `TG_PEER_B` was suppressed with `denied_by_send_policy` / `send_policy_peer_mismatch` and zero Telegram API send calls, so `TG_PEER_B` received no Telegram message. Current head additionally preserves legacy queued explicit sends by replaying missing `sendPolicyMode` as `explicit`, covered by `src/infra/outbound/delivery-queue.recovery.test.ts`.

- **What was not tested:** The real Telegram send itself was captured on proof head `e7b028ba5aa2c7444937deb020e838e130945f70`, not repeated after the final `86f2586a` recovery/footprint commit. That final commit is limited to legacy queue replay compatibility and explicit-send footprint cleanup, while the current PR body and head now pass the required `Real behavior proof` gate at `26369003419`.

### Config under test

`session.sendPolicy` denied direct Telegram sends when the outbound peer did not equal the trusted inbound peer candidates:

```json
{
  "default": "allow",
  "rules": [
    {
      "action": "deny",
      "match": {
        "allOf": [
          { "channel": "telegram" },
          { "chatType": "direct" },
          { "peerEquals": "inboundPeer", "invert": true }
        ]
      }
    }
  ]
}
```

### Proof verdict

- Same-peer Telegram durable send to `TG_PEER_A` was delivered.
- Wrong-peer durable outbound attempt to `TG_PEER_B` was suppressed by `session.sendPolicy`.
- Suppression included `denied_by_send_policy` and `send_policy_peer_mismatch`.
- The wrong-peer case recorded zero Telegram API send calls, so `TG_PEER_B` received no Telegram message.
- Token, real chat id, phone number, username, and message content beyond the proof marker are redacted.

target.title: feat(cron): add agent-turn mode for failure alerts
target.body:
Closes #56521

## What

Adds a new `agent-turn` mode for cron job `failureAlert` that injects the error as a system event into the agent session instead of sending an outbound notification (Telegram/etc).

## Why

When cron jobs fail, the current `announce` mode sends a notification to the user's chat. But the agent (main session) is unaware of the failure — the human has to relay the error manually.

With `agent-turn` mode, the failure alert triggers the agent directly via `enqueueSystemEvent` + `requestHeartbeatNow`, allowing the agent to:
- Diagnose the error autonomously
- Attempt a fix if possible
- Report the outcome to the human

## How

- New `failureAlert.mode: 'agent-turn'` option
- In `emitFailureAlert`, when mode is `agent-turn`, bypasses `sendCronFailureAlert` and uses the existing `enqueueSystemEvent` + `requestHeartbeatNow` path
- CLI: `openclaw cron edit <id> --failure-alert-mode agent-turn`

## Usage

```bash
openclaw cron edit <job-id> --failure-alert-mode agent-turn
```

Or in config:
```json
{
  "failureAlert": {
    "mode": "agent-turn",
    "after": 1
  }
}
```

target.title: fix(cron): prevent one-shot at jobs from re-triggering after completion
target.body:
## Problem

Fixes #56488

`kind: "at"` cron jobs with `deleteAfterRun: true` fire repeatedly instead of once. The reporter observed a one-shot Sparkle insight job firing 7+ times overnight, each spawning an isolated agent session that generated content and posted to Discord.

## Root Cause

`isRunnableJob` has a `skipAtIfAlreadyRan` guard (line 753) that prevents completed one-shot jobs from re-triggering. But this guard is only enabled when called from `runMissedJobs` (the startup catch-up path, line 873).

The normal `onTimer` tick (line 595) and `runDueJobs` (line 1006) call `collectRunnableJobs` WITHOUT `skipAtIfAlreadyRan`. So completed "at" jobs with past-due `nextRunAtMs` are treated as runnable on every scheduler tick, because `nowMs >= nextRunAtMs` is always true for a past timestamp.

```
onTimer tick:
  collectRunnableJobs(state, now)  ← no skipAtIfAlreadyRan
  → isRunnableJob:
    → skipAtIfAlreadyRan? NO (not passed)
    → nowMs >= nextRunAtMs? YES (past timestamp)
    → return true  ← re-triggers!

runMissedJobs (startup):
  collectRunnableJobs(state, now, { skipAtIfAlreadyRan: true })
  → isRunnableJob:
    → skipAtIfAlreadyRan? YES
    → lastStatus === "ok"? YES
    → return false  ← correctly skipped
```

## Fix

Pass `skipAtIfAlreadyRan: true` in both `onTimer` and `runDueJobs`, matching the startup catch-up path behavior. The guard implementation already handles all edge cases (error retries with `nextRun > lastRun`).

target.title: fix: add exponential backoff for secret provider resolution at startup
target.body:
## Problem

Fixes #56217

When a secret provider (e.g., 1Password `op read`) fails transiently at startup, the gateway crashes immediately. If the process manager (launchd/systemd) restarts it, each restart fires N `op read` calls that fail again:

```
Gateway starts → op read × 6 providers → 1 fails → CRASH
  ↓ (launchd restarts after 30s)
Gateway starts → op read × 6 providers → 1 fails → CRASH
  ↓ (launchd restarts after 30s)
  ... (281,000+ requests in 24 hours)
```

Once the rate limit is hit, ALL 1Password API access is blocked across ALL services on the account for up to 24 hours. Not just OpenClaw, everything.

## Root Cause

`activateRuntimeSecrets` (server.impl.ts:486) throws a fatal error on ANY secret resolution failure at startup, with zero retry and zero backoff. Transient failures (network blip, 1Password CLI timeout, temporary rate limit) are treated identically to permanent failures (bad token, missing secret). The process exits, the OS restarts it, and the cycle repeats.

The non-startup path (reload/restart-check) already handles failures gracefully by falling back to the last-known-good snapshot. But at startup there's no last-known-good, so any failure is fatal.

## Fix

Added `attemptResolve()` with exponential backoff around `prepareSecretsRuntimeSnapshot` at startup:

- **3 retries** with delays of **2s, 4s, 8s** (14s total before giving up)
- Each retry attempt logs `[SECRETS_STARTUP_RETRY]` with attempt number and delay
- Only applies when `reason === "startup"` (reload/restart-check paths unchanged)
- After exhausting retries, the error message includes actionable guidance about checking service account tokens and rate limits

Transient failures (network blip, socket timeout, temporary rate limit) are likely to succeed on retry 2 or 3 without triggering a full process restart. For permanent failures (revoked token, deleted secret), the retries exhaust quickly (14s) and the gateway still fails with a clear error.

## Impact

With the reporter's setup (6 providers, 30s restart interval):
- **Before:** ~720 `op` calls/hour during crash-loop (0 backoff)
- **After:** Transient failures recovered in-process without restart. Permanent failures add only 14s delay before the (now-inevitable) crash, reducing restart frequency by ~30%

target.title: feat(msteams): add native plugin interactivity parity
target.body:
## Summary

DO NOT MERGE YET -- for review

- Problem: OpenClaw's Teams channel could send messages/cards, but the native plugin interactivity path still stopped short of Teams. The Codex app-server Teams work therefore had to rely on a command bridge instead of the same core plugin/runtime flow used by first-class channels.
- Why it matters: I am working on this so Teams support for the Codex app-server path is grounded in real OpenClaw channel support, not a partial workaround. Without these core changes, Teams actions, binding flows, message edits, and channel-scoped Graph operations do not have the same native foundation as Discord/Telegram-style plugin integrations.
- What changed: this PR adds native Teams plugin interactive dispatch in `src/plugins/interactive.ts` and `src/plugins/interactive-dispatch-adapters.ts`, adds the Teams runtime surface in `src/plugins/runtime/runtime-msteams.ts`, `src/plugins/runtime/runtime-msteams-typing.ts`, and `src/plugins/runtime/runtime-msteams-ops.runtime.ts`, wires Teams invoke handling through `extensions/msteams/src/plugin-interactions.ts` and `extensions/msteams/src/monitor-handler.ts`, extends command/target normalization in `src/plugins/commands.ts` and `src/channels/plugins/target-parsing.ts`, and fixes Teams Graph/edit/allowlist correctness in `extensions/msteams/src/send.ts`, `extensions/msteams/src/graph.ts`, `extensions/msteams/src/graph-messages.ts`, `extensions/msteams/src/graph-thread.ts`, `extensions/msteams/src/resolve-allowlist.ts`, and `extensions/msteams/src/policy.ts`.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): this does not try to fix unrelated latest-`main` agent/skills TypeScript failures, and it does not claim live-tenant validation for every Teams topology (for example shared-channel behavior still needs real-environment confirmation outside this PR).

## Change Type

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes: N/A
- Related: cross-repo Teams parity work in `openclaw-codex-app-server`
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History

- Root cause: Teams support in core had been built around message/card sending and text-command handling, but not the native plugin interactive dispatch/runtime path. That left Codex app-server Teams support depending on a hidden command bridge instead of the same first-class interactive surface exposed to other channels.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was no Teams-native seam/integration coverage for plugin invoke handling, source-message edits/clears, Graph channel id translation, or fail-closed allowlist resolution.
- Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): the current code shape reflected the earlier minimal Teams MVP, where `src/plugins/interactive.ts` and runtime channel exposure were intentionally limited and Teams only had the smaller send-oriented surface.
- Why this regressed now: the cross-repo Codex Teams work pushed beyond that MVP and exposed the remaining OpenClaw core gaps.
- If unknown, what was ruled out: N/A

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `extensions/msteams/src/monitor-handler.plugin-interactions.test.ts`
  - `extensions/msteams/src/graph-messages.test.ts`
  - `extensions/msteams/src/send.test.ts`
  - `extensions/msteams/src/resolve-allowlist.test.ts`
  - `extensions/msteams/src/policy.test.ts`
  - `src/plugins/interactive.test.ts`
  - `src/plugins/runtime/index.test.ts`
  - `src/plugins/commands.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: Teams invoke payloads dispatch through the native plugin interactive handler, reply/follow-up/edit/clear flows update the expected Teams message/card surface, command/binding targets normalize consistently, Graph channel operations resolve the correct team/channel ids, and allowlist failures fail closed instead of widening access.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the failures crossed the Teams adapter, plugin interactive core, and Graph-backed Teams operations; narrower unit tests would miss the integration seams that were actually broken.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): the files above now cover the main Teams seams.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Teams plugin actions now go through native OpenClaw plugin interactivity instead of relying on a command-only bridge.
- Teams plugin replies/follow-ups/edit-source/clear-actions now preserve better source-message behavior for Adaptive Card flows.
- Teams plugin command binding and explicit target parsing now normalize `teams:` / `msteams:` conversation targets consistently.
- Teams channel-scoped Graph operations use the resolved Graph team id path more safely.
- Teams allowlist resolution now fails closed when primary-channel lookup cannot safely resolve a usable runtime key.

## Diagram

```text
Before:
Teams Action.Submit -> Teams-specific command bridge -> plugin workaround -> partial updates

After:
Teams Action.Submit -> extensions/msteams/src/plugin-interactions.ts
                  -> src/plugins/interactive.ts
                  -> plugin handler/runtime.channel.msteams
                  -> native reply/edit/typing/channel actions
```

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:
  - This PR gives plugins a new trusted `runtime.channel.msteams` surface for Teams-native operations and adds Teams-native interactive dispatch. The risk is widening what a plugin can do in Teams or routing to the wrong target. Mitigations in this PR are explicit runtime typing in `src/plugins/runtime/types-channel.ts`, targeted runtime wiring instead of broad generic exposure, conversation-scoped interactive handling in `extensions/msteams/src/plugin-interactions.ts`, Graph team-id resolution fixes in `extensions/msteams/src/graph-thread.ts` / `extensions/msteams/src/graph.ts`, and fail-closed allowlist behavior in `extensions/msteams/src/resolve-allowlist.ts` / `extensions/msteams/src/policy.ts`.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: local `pnpm` workspace checkout
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Microsoft Teams (`extensions/msteams`)
- Relevant config (redacted): local dev config only; no secrets included in this PR

### Steps

1. Enable the Teams channel and a plugin that uses OpenClaw interactive handlers/runtime operations.
2. Trigger a Teams plugin interaction that needs source-message edits, follow-ups, binding, or Teams-native runtime actions.
3. Exercise channel-scoped Teams paths that resolve Graph ids and allowlist-target normalization.

### Expected

- Teams interactive actions route through the native plugin handler path.
- Teams runtime operations are available through `runtime.channel.msteams`.
- Teams message/card edit flows keep the intended source-card behavior.
- Teams channel Graph operations resolve the correct team/channel path.
- Unsafe Teams allowlist fallbacks do not widen access.

### Actual

- This branch implements those paths in code and verifies them with the Teams-focused tests listed below.
- After rebasing onto latest `main`, repo-wide `pnpm check` is blocked by unrelated `src/agents/**` TypeScript failures already present on top of `main`; those are out of scope for this PR.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Concrete issues fixed in this branch and covered by passing tests/review loops:

- Teams plugin interactions could not run through the native OpenClaw interactive dispatch path.
- Teams source-message update paths could clear or duplicate replies incorrectly.
- Teams channel Graph operations could use the wrong team identifier for channel-scoped requests.
- Teams allowlist resolution briefly risked widening access when primary-channel lookup failed; this branch keeps that path fail-closed.

Evidence run locally during this work:

- `pnpm test -- extensions/msteams/src/graph-messages.test.ts extensions/msteams/src/monitor-handler.plugin-interactions.test.ts extensions/msteams/src/resolve-allowlist.test.ts extensions/msteams/src/policy.test.ts`
- Earlier Teams-scoped validation on this branch also included:
  - `extensions/msteams/src/send.test.ts`
  - `src/plugins/interactive.test.ts`
  - `src/plugins/runtime/index.test.ts`
  - `src/plugins/commands.test.ts`
- Before the rebase to latest `main`, this branch also passed:
  - `pnpm check`
  - `pnpm build`
  - `pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check`

## Human Verification

NOT YET!


- Verified scenarios:
  - reviewed the Teams interactive dispatch flow from `extensions/msteams/src/plugin-interactions.ts` into `src/plugins/interactive.ts`
  - verified the new Teams runtime surface and SDK baseline updates
  - verified Teams target parsing / command binding normalization
  - verified Teams Graph id resolution and allowlist behavior through targeted tests and review-fix loops
  - rebased the branch onto latest `origin/main` and resolved the resulting conflicts
- Edge cases checked:
  - Teams source-message edit vs clear-card behavior
  - Teams interaction fallthrough when a handler declines handling
  - Graph team-id recovery / paging behavior
  - fail-closed allowlist resolution when primary-channel lookup is unavailable
- What you did **not** verify:
  - no live Microsoft Teams tenant verification in this PR
  - no shared/private-channel live validation beyond code/test coverage
  - no attempt to fix unrelated latest-`main` `src/agents/**` failures

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Teams interactive/message-edit behavior can differ from Telegram/Discord because Adaptive Cards and Teams invoke semantics are different.
  - Mitigation: keep Teams-specific behavior isolated under `extensions/msteams/**`, cover the adapter seams with tests, and preserve explanatory comments in the non-obvious parsing/Graph-resolution paths.
- Risk: Graph team/channel id translation could still behave differently in real tenants than in mocked tests.
  - Mitigation: the code now handles Graph-usable ids directly, pages through group lookup fallback, and fails closed when it cannot safely resolve a usable target.
- Risk: exposing Teams runtime actions to plugins could widen the trusted plugin surface.
  - Mitigation: the surface is explicit and typed in the runtime channel layer rather than being an unbounded Teams SDK export.

target.title: [Feature]: Channel Broker Phase 2A conformance harness
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds the Phase 2A Channel Broker conformance baseline on top of #86096.
- Extends `openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-broker` with inbound normalization, dedupe-key, provider capability normalization, and platform capability checks.
- Adds bundled broker conformance coverage for durable inbound receive, durable final send receipts, retryable receipt failures, preview/progress capability gates, and `/verbose`/tool-message `channelData`.

## Rollout Context

Closes #86109
Related #86108
Related #86105
Stacked on #86096

This PR intentionally does not migrate Telegram, Discord, or Slack routing. Native defaults stay unchanged until the Phase 2B/2C/2D proofs land behind opt-in broker routing.

For review, the focused stack diff is:
https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-protocol...feat/channel-broker-conformance

## Real Behavior Proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** The broker conformance baseline normalizes inbound events, builds durable dedupe keys, evaluates preview/progress capabilities, and sends outbound requests through the bundled broker lifecycle without platform-specific core branches.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`, using the Channel Broker plugin and SDK from the stacked rollout branch on May 24, 2026.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a `node --import tsx --input-type=module` terminal proof that imported `src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.ts`, created a Telegram inbound event, built the durable dedupe key, evaluated Discord preview/progress capabilities, and sent broker outbound requests through `channelBrokerPlugin.message.send.text`.
- **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output excerpt from that local command:

```json
{
  "inboundDedupeKey": "acme:bot-main:telegram:live-proof-telegram-1",
  "previewProgressSupported": true
}
```

- **Observed result after fix:** The live SDK/plugin path produced the expected durable inbound key and capability decision outside the Vitest harness, proving the Phase 2A contract surface can carry the normalized lifecycle facts that later channel migrations consume.
- **What was not tested:** Live provider network traffic and the full local suite; those remain covered by later provider proofs and GitHub Actions.

### Behavior

The broker contract now has a conformance harness for the lifecycle paths that regress most often across maintained chat channels: inbound normalization/dedupe, durable final sends, provider receipts, retry/failure propagation, preview/progress capability checks, and `/verbose` tool-message metadata.

### Environment

- Local checkout: `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`
- Branch: `feat/channel-broker-conformance`
- Head: `55adfa7851 Add channel broker conformance baseline`

### Steps

1. Normalized a synthetic Telegram inbound event through the SDK and accepted it through the durable inbound receive journal.
2. Replayed the same dedupe key while pending and after completion to prove duplicate/redelivery handling.
3. Sent a synthetic Slack final message through the bundled broker runtime and verified provider receipt commit material.
4. Returned a retryable Discord provider receipt and verified the send fails with structured receipt context.
5. Built preview/finalize broker requests and asserted Discord preview/progress support through declared capabilities.
6. Passed `/verbose` tool-message policy through broker `channelData` without platform-specific branching.

### Evidence

- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_INCLUDE_FILE=/Volumes/LEXAR/Codex/channel-broker-vitest-include.json pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts`
  - 1 file passed, 7 tests passed.
- `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/secret-contract.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts`
  - 3 files passed, 10 tests passed.
- `pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json`
  - passed.
- `pnpm tsgo:core:test`
  - passed.
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.ts src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/outbound.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts`
  - passed.
- `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/channel-broker --format unix`
  - passed.
- `git diff --check`
  - passed.

### Observed Result

The SDK helpers normalize provider payloads without dropping native metadata, the bundled plugin refuses non-sent provider receipts as durable-send failures, and conformance tests prove the current broker path can represent the Phase 2 lifecycle requirements without changing user-visible channel defaults.

### Not Tested

- Full local suite. Per `/Volumes/LEXAR` policy, broad validation is left to GitHub Actions.
- Live Telegram, Discord, or Slack provider traffic. Those are intentionally scoped to Phase 2B/2C/2D.

## Current Upstream Gate Context

PR #86096 currently has one unrelated `checks-node-agentic-agents` CI timeout in `src/agents/model-catalog-visibility.test.ts`; the failed test is outside this broker stack. I attempted to rerun the failed job, but GitHub blocked it because this account does not have repository admin rights.

target.title: [Feature]: Channel Broker Phase 2B Telegram migration proof
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds the Phase 2B Telegram broker routing proof on top of #86133 and #86096.
- Normalizes Telegram topic shorthand in the broker plugin, including `broker:telegram:<chat>:topic:<id>` and numeric `<chat>:<topic>` targets.
- Canonicalizes outbound broker session routes to `telegram:<chat>?threadId=<id>` so topic routing is stable without importing native Telegram internals.

## Rollout Context

Closes #86110
Related #86108
Related #86105
Stacked on #86133
Stacked on #86096

Native Telegram defaults stay unchanged. Existing target-prefix ownership still keeps `telegram:` owned by the native Telegram plugin when that plugin is installed; this PR proves the opt-in broker route through `broker:telegram:...` and the broker alias path when native Telegram is absent.

For review, the focused stack diff is:
https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-conformance...feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing

## Real Behavior Proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Telegram topic targets can route through `channel-broker` and become provider requests with canonical `telegram` platform, channel conversation id, and topic `threadId`.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`, using the Channel Broker plugin and SDK from the stacked rollout branch on May 24, 2026.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a `node --import tsx --input-type=module` terminal proof that called `channelBrokerPlugin.message.send.text` with `to: "broker:telegram:-1001234567890:topic:42"` and captured the provider request passed to the configured broker runtime.
- **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output excerpt from that local command:

```json
{
  "label": "telegram-topic",
  "messageId": "native-telegram-topic",
  "platform": "telegram",
  "conversation": {
    "id": "-1001234567890",
    "type": "channel",
    "threadId": "42"
  },
  "requirements": {
    "text": true,
    "thread": true
  }
}
```

- **Observed result after fix:** The live plugin path normalized Telegram topic shorthand into the broker provider request shape without importing native Telegram extension internals.
- **What was not tested:** Live Telegram Bot API traffic and the full local suite; this PR stays scoped to opt-in broker routing proof.

### Behavior

OpenClaw can route a Telegram topic through `channel-broker` without Telegram extension code. The broker receives a normalized platform `telegram`, conversation id, and thread id; OpenClaw keeps the session route canonical and leaves native Telegram prefix ownership intact.

### Environment

- Local checkout: `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`
- Branch: `feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing`
- Head: `40a181b5b2 Add Telegram broker routing proof`

### Steps

1. Sent a synthetic message to `broker:telegram:-1001234567890:topic:42` through `channelBrokerPlugin.message.send.text`.
2. Verified the provider request used `platform: "telegram"`, `conversation.id: "-1001234567890"`, and `conversation.threadId: "42"`.
3. Resolved the outbound session route for the same target.
4. Verified the route canonicalized to `telegram:-1001234567890?threadId=42` and preserved channel chat semantics.

### Evidence

- `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts`
  - 2 files passed, 10 tests passed.
- `pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json`
  - passed.
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check extensions/channel-broker/src/target.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts`
  - passed.
- `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/channel-broker --format unix`
  - passed.
- `git diff --check`
  - passed.

### Observed Result

Telegram topic routing is represented by broker-owned target normalization and provider request semantics, not by core importing or calling native Telegram extension internals.

### Not Tested

- Live Telegram Bot API traffic; this PR is the opt-in broker migration proof, not a live provider connector.
- Full local suite. Per `/Volumes/LEXAR` policy, broad validation is left to GitHub Actions.

## Current Upstream Gate Context

PR #86096 currently has one unrelated `checks-node-agentic-agents` CI timeout in `src/agents/model-catalog-visibility.test.ts`; the failed test is outside this broker stack. I attempted to rerun the failed job, but GitHub blocked it because this account does not have repository admin rights.

target.title: feat(memory-core): Memory Privacy Audit + Encrypted Backup
target.body:
## Summary
Build the first contribution as **privacy visibility plus encrypted backup**, not live encrypted memory. The feature adds a safe CLI workflow that audits where OpenClaw stores memory-related data, flags privacy risks, and supports encrypted export/import of memory artifacts.

### Key Changes
- Add CLI commands:
  - `openclaw memory privacy audit`
  - `openclaw memory export --encrypted --out <file>.age`
  - `openclaw memory import --encrypted --in <file>.age --target <dir>`
- Audit reports:
  - memory workspace path
  - `MEMORY.md`, `memory/*.md`, `DREAMS.md`, `memory/.dreams/`, and dreaming reports
  - SQLite/vector index path and size
  - embedding provider classification: local, remote, none, or unknown
  - Active Memory transcript persistence state
  - plaintext memory surfaces and backup risks
- Encrypted backup:
  - export creates a tar archive of selected memory artifacts, then encrypts it
  - output uses the `.age` format via the TypeScript `age-encryption` package
  - import decrypts into an explicit target directory
  - import refuses to overwrite existing files by default
  - no live memory files are changed during export

### Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Adds memory privacy visibility plus encrypted backup/restore at rest. This proof covers `openclaw memory privacy audit`, non-empty `openclaw memory export --encrypted`, and `openclaw memory import --encrypted`, after the symlink hardening and CI fixes in commit `b7749a4f36`.

**Real environment tested:** Isolated local lab only: `/Volumes/Untitled/openclaw-privacy-lab/openclaw`, with `OPENCLAW_HOME` pointed at `/Volumes/Untitled/openclaw-privacy-lab/proof-20260512-190512/home`. The real production OpenClaw home `/Users/anandsagar/.openclaw` was not used.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
```console
$ export OPENCLAW_HOME=/Volumes/Untitled/openclaw-privacy-lab/proof-20260512-190512/home
$ export OPENCLAW_MEMORY_BACKUP_PASSPHRASE='proof-passphrase'
$ node openclaw.mjs memory privacy audit
$ node openclaw.mjs memory export --encrypted --out /Volumes/Untitled/openclaw-privacy-lab/proof-20260512-190512/memory-backup.age
$ node openclaw.mjs memory import --encrypted --in /Volumes/Untitled/openclaw-privacy-lab/proof-20260512-190512/memory-backup.age --target /Volumes/Untitled/openclaw-privacy-lab/proof-20260512-190512/restore
$ shasum -a 256 /Volumes/Untitled/openclaw-privacy-lab/proof-20260512-190512/home/.openclaw/workspace/MEMORY.md /Volumes/Untitled/openclaw-privacy-lab/proof-20260512-190512/restore/MEMORY.md /Volumes/Untitled/openclaw-privacy-lab/proof-20260512-190512/home/.openclaw/workspace/DREAMS.md /Volumes/Untitled/openclaw-privacy-lab/proof-20260512-190512/restore/DREAMS.md /Volumes/Untitled/openclaw-privacy-lab/proof-20260512-190512/home/.openclaw/workspace/memory/2026-05-12.md /Volumes/Untitled/openclaw-privacy-lab/proof-20260512-190512/restore/memory/2026-05-12.md
```

**Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from the isolated lab run:

target.title: feat(imessage): support thumb approval reactions
target.body:
## Summary

- Mirrors openclaw#85477 (WhatsApp) for the iMessage channel: 👍 (Like tapback) resolves an approval as `allow-once`, 👎 (Dislike) resolves as `deny`, and `allow-always` stays on the manual `/approve <id> allow-always` text fallback.
- Forwarding-mode approval prompts (`channels.imessage.allowFrom`-gated) are delivered to iMessage via the regular outbound send, get the canonical `React with:` hint appended, and their messageId is registered against the approval id with TTL + persistent backing.
- Native-runtime adapter, channel capability, and `registerChannelRuntimeContext` call are wired up for session-mode delivery; targets-mode forwarding works in parallel.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: iMessage can deliver native exec/plugin approvals and resolve them via 👍 / 👎 tapbacks.
Real environment tested: Live Lobster gateway (macOS, Node 24, branch `codex/imessage-thumb-approvals` deployed at commit on top of `main`). Approval target was a real iMessage allowlist entry; sender identity redacted as `+1XXXXXXXXXX` below.
Exact steps or command run after this patch:
- `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile`
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.extensions.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/extensions.tsbuildinfo` (passes)
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/imessage/src/approval-auth.test.ts extensions/imessage/src/approval-reactions.test.ts extensions/imessage/src/approval-handler.runtime.test.ts extensions/imessage/src/approval-native.test.ts` (40 passed)
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/imessage` (411 passed)
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1` over all touched files (clean)
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs` over all touched files (clean)
- `pnpm build` (clean)
- Live exercise: configured `approvals.plugin.targets` with `{channel: imessage, to: <approver>}` and `{channel: telegram, to: <fallback>}`; triggered a `fastmail_get_email` plugin approval from `lobster-mail`; received the prompt on iMessage; tapped 👍 → gateway recorded `imessage: approval reaction resolved id=plugin:8d5075f3-… sender=*** decision=allow-once`; resolved-update message arrived on the same iMessage thread. Repeated with a second `fastmail_get_email` approval and tapped 👎 → `imessage: approval reaction resolved id=plugin:eb76a7e8-… sender=*** decision=deny`. Telegram delivery remained healthy throughout as the parallel target.
Evidence after fix: All focused tests pass (40 new approval-specific cases plus the full 411-case iMessage suite). Gateway log on live deploy: `telegram/native-approvals: resolved plugin:8d5075f3-… with allow-once` → `plugin.approval.resolve 1308ms ✓` → `imessage: approval reaction resolved … decision=allow-once`; equivalent log line for `deny`.
Observed result after fix: Approval prompts arrive on iMessage with the new `👍 Allow Once / 👎 Deny` hint, `ID:` header, and `/approve <id> …` text fallback; a tapback within the 120 s expiry resolves the approval at the gateway with the matching decision and emits a follow-up update message on the same iMessage thread; out-of-allowlist senders are denied; `allow-always` continues to require the manual text command.
What was not tested: Crabbox / Testbox broad suites, multi-account iMessage with distinct allowFrom-per-account, and the legacy text-tapback path (\"Liked \\\"…\\\"\" plain-text reactions from older Apple clients), which is documented as a known limitation because no `reacted_to_guid` is available on that path.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- iMessage approval prompts now show `👍 Allow Once` and `👎 Deny` as reaction choices. Tapping resolves the approval against the gateway.
- `allow-always` stays on the manual `/approve <id> allow-always` reply path.
- The `/approve` text command is now authorized against `channels.imessage.allowFrom` (the approver list) rather than the broader DM allowlist when `allowFrom` is non-empty. Operators with a non-empty `allowFrom` who relied on the DM allowlist for `/approve` access need to ensure every approver is listed in `allowFrom`. When `allowFrom` is empty the legacy \"implicit same-chat fallback\" still applies and `/approve` continues to authorize anyone the DM allowlist permits.

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? Yes — reactions on approval prompts can resolve exec/plugin approvals via the gateway when the reacting handle is in `channels.imessage.allowFrom` (or `\"*\"`).
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No.
- New/changed network calls? No (uses existing imsg / gateway transports).
- Command/tool execution surface changed? Yes — exec/plugin approvals can now be approved from iMessage.
- Data access scope changed? No.
- Mitigations: delivery is gated by top-level `approvals.exec` / `approvals.plugin`; reactions require an explicit approver in `channels.imessage.allowFrom` (the reaction-resolver short-circuits with an explicit denial otherwise); cross-device `is_from_me=true` tapbacks are ignored so the bot cannot self-approve; the reaction binding is cleared on a successful resolve to prevent toggle-replay; non-`ApprovalNotFoundError` failures now log at warn so a silent gateway outage is visible.

## Regression Test Plan

- `extensions/imessage/src/approval-auth.test.ts` — approver normalization, wildcard `\"*\"`, group/conversation prefixes rejected, lowercase email canonicalization, `imessage:`/`sms:`/`auto:` service-prefix stripping.
- `extensions/imessage/src/approval-reactions.test.ts` — thumbs-only hint rendering, allow-always not exposed as a reaction, DM key-mismatch resolution (send registers `handle:`, inbound looks up by chat_guid first), `is_from_me` guard, removed-tapback ignored, clear-on-success behavior, `$`-escape regression cover for approval id placeholder substitution, regex tightening (require canonical `ID:` header before binding).
- `extensions/imessage/src/approval-handler.runtime.test.ts` — pending exec/plugin payload rendering, target normalization for handle / chat_guid / chat_id / chat_identifier.
- `extensions/imessage/src/approval-native.test.ts` — capability gating (top-level `approvals.exec`/`approvals.plugin` toggles), session-mode origin/approver-DM resolution, group origin rejection without approvers, render hooks.
- `src/auto-reply/reply/commands-approve.test.ts` and `src/infra/channel-approval-auth.test.ts` — manual `/approve` command authorization fallback remains intact for other channels.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Mostly. Existing iMessage configs continue to work; the only behavior change is the `/approve` command auth tightening described above when `channels.imessage.allowFrom` is non-empty. Documented in `docs/channels/imessage.md`.
- Config/env changes? No new iMessage-specific approval field. iMessage continues to use top-level `approvals.exec` / `approvals.plugin` for delivery gating and `channels.imessage.allowFrom` for approver authorization.
- Migration needed? Operators with a non-empty `channels.imessage.allowFrom` should confirm every desired `/approve` operator is listed in it (typically already the case).

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: fix(slack): keep DM thread turns out of active steering
target.body:
## Summary

- Preserve OpenClaw's stable Slack DM `SessionKey` contract for top-level DMs and ordinary DM thread replies.
- Track routed Slack thread ids on active reply operations, including agent-runner-owned, dispatch-owned, and queued follow-up operations.
- Suppress active-run steering and dispatch admission blocking only for Slack direct-message turns that carry Slack routed thread metadata and target a different Slack thread, so non-Slack IM steering/admission behavior remains unchanged.

## Verification

- Confirmed latest `origin/main` at `1d1a7c26d8867fdfa98c97d8b510ad0ce1951858` still does not include this fix.
- Watched the original Slack regression fail before the fix: `expected true to be false` for `call.shouldSteer`, proving the second Slack DM thread would still steer into the active run.
- Watched the dispatch admission regression fail before the latest fix: `Slack routed thread was blocked by the active reply operation`, matching the live Slack symptom where B was skipped as `reply-operation-active`.
- Watched the non-Slack compatibility regression fail before the Slack-only scoping change: `expected false to be true` for `call.shouldSteer`, proving the generic route-thread guard changed existing non-Slack same-session steering.
- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=2 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts -- -t "different Slack DM routed thread|non-Slack routed direct turns|records routed Slack thread id" --test-timeout 5000 --hook-timeout 5000` - 1 file passed, 156 tests passed.
- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=2 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/slack/src/monitor/message-handler/prepare.thread-session-key.test.ts extensions/slack/src/monitor/message-handler/prepare.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/get-reply-run.media-only.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/followup-runner.test.ts` - 5 files passed, 378 tests passed, duration 19.71s.
- `git diff --check FETCH_HEAD` - passed.
- Attempted `node scripts/crabbox-wrapper.mjs run slack-dm-thread-isolation-check-changed --provider blacksmith-testbox --shell -- "pnpm check:changed"`; it did not start because this local environment has no usable `crabbox` binary (`selected binary failed basic --version/--help sanity checks`).

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: Two overlapping top-level Slack DMs from the same Slack sender should keep the stable DM `SessionKey`, but the second top-level Slack thread must not be steered into, or blocked behind dispatch admission for, the first active run. Ordinary DM thread follow-up should continue to work in the original Slack thread. The steering and dispatch-admission guards are Slack-scoped, so non-Slack same-session direct-message turns keep their prior behavior even when their transport thread ids differ.
- Real environment tested: Real Slack workspace `w1773415900-eqa641873.slack.com`, OpenClaw source checkout `d0d5a354e77e5650f0ff3126eea42cae04a008e3`, foreground gateway started from this branch with `OPENCLAW_HOME=/tmp/openclaw-pr85904-live-root WORKSPACE=/tmp/openclaw-pr85904-live-workspace node scripts/run-node.mjs gateway run --port 18790 --verbose --ws-log compact`. The normal installed gateway was stopped before the smoke and restarted after proof capture.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Started the PR gateway with a temporary redacted proof config using Slack Socket Mode, `replyToMode=all`, and `allowBots=true` because the available automated Slack acceptance sender posts app-authored Slack messages. The OpenClaw bot under test was a different bot id, so this did not exercise self-message handling. Sent top-level DM A and top-level DM B from the same sender in the same DM channel about 1.00s apart, then sent ordinary follow-up D inside B's Slack thread. After the dispatch-admission repair, ran the current-head local regression set above.
- Evidence after fix: Redacted Slack API and gateway evidence from the live workspace:

```text
Gateway under test: OpenClaw 2026.5.24 (d0d5a35)
Loaded channel runtime: Slack Socket Mode connected; octoclaw-runtime was not loaded in this proof gateway.
Workspace: w1773415900-eqa641873.slack.com
Sender: user=U...9Z bot_id=B...N2
OpenClaw bot: user=U...CQ bot_id=B...57
DM channel: D...QL
Nonce: oc-proof-8e6bc0a0

Top-level A sent: ts=1779642174.068849 thread_ts=1779642174.068849
Top-level B sent: ts=1779642175.071749 thread_ts=1779642175.071749
Delta between A and B sends: 1.002900s

Gateway current-head routing evidence:
- Slack B inbound used stable SessionKey agent:main:slack:default:direct:u0...9z.
- While A was active, gateway logged: dispatch-from-config: allowing Slack routed thread 1779642175.071749 while agent:main:slack:default:direct:u0...9z has an active reply operation in another Slack thread.
- No `reply-operation-active` skip occurred for B on current head d0d5a354e77e.

A thread replies:
- user=U...9Z bot_id=B...N2 ts=1779642174.068849 thread_ts=1779642174.068849 text="[oc-proof-8e6bc0a0] top-level DM A... FINAL-A=oc-proof-8e6bc0a0-A"
- user=U...CQ bot_id=B...57 ts=1779642207.663989 thread_ts=1779642174.068849 text="... oc-proof-8e6bc0a0-A ..."

B thread replies:
- user=U...9Z bot_id=B...N2 ts=1779642175.071749 thread_ts=1779642175.071749 text="[oc-proof-8e6bc0a0] top-level DM B... FINAL-B=oc-proof-8e6bc0a0-B"
- user=U...CQ bot_id=B...57 ts=1779642212.717829 thread_ts=1779642175.071749 text="... FINAL-B=oc-proof-8e6bc0a0-B"

Ordinary DM thread follow-up in B:
- user=U...9Z bot_id=B...N2 ts=1779642234.122779 thread_ts=1779642175.071749 text="[oc-proof-8e6bc0a0] ordinary DM thread follow-up D in B thread... FINAL-D=oc-proof-8e6bc0a0-D"
- user=U...CQ bot_id=B...57 ts=1779642247.227079 thread_ts=1779642175.071749 text="... FINAL-D=oc-proof-8e6bc0a0-D"

Local compatibility proof after Slack-only scoping:
- Slack direct dispatch turn with MessageThreadId 501.000 vs active routeThreadId 500.000: reached reply resolution and sent a final reply.
- Non-Slack Telegram direct dispatch turn with MessageThreadId 501.000 vs active routeThreadId 500.000: stayed blocked behind the active reply operation and did not call reply resolution.
- Slack direct run-prep turn with MessageThreadId 501.000 vs active routeThreadId 500.000: shouldSteer=false, shouldFollowup=true.
- Slack direct run-prep turn with TransportThreadId 501.000 vs active routeThreadId 500.000: shouldSteer=false, shouldFollowup=true.
- Telegram direct run-prep turn with MessageThreadId 43 vs active routeThreadId 42: shouldSteer=true, shouldFollowup=true.
```

- Observed result after fix: The two overlapping top-level Slack DMs did not collapse into one Slack reply thread and B was not skipped as `reply-operation-active`. A received an OpenClaw reply in A's Slack thread, B received an OpenClaw reply in B's Slack thread, and the later B-thread follow-up continued in B's thread. The final active-run routing and dispatch-admission guards apply only when `ChatType=direct`, routed thread metadata exists, and `Provider`, `Surface`, or `OriginatingChannel` resolves to `slack`.
- What was not tested: The automated sender available in this environment posts Slack app-authored messages, so the temporary proof config enabled `allowBots=true`. This live proof still exercises the real Slack Socket Mode transport, real Slack thread ids, and the PR checkout's active-run routing and dispatch-admission boundaries; it does not prove Slack's human-user token UX separately. The local Crabbox/Testbox `pnpm check:changed` gate was attempted but could not start because the local `crabbox` binary is missing or unusable.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): Before the fix, the same local runtime scenario failed with `AssertionError: expected true to be false` at `src/auto-reply/reply/get-reply-run.media-only.test.ts`, showing the same-session/different-thread second turn would steer into the active run. Before the latest dispatch-admission fix, live current-head proof failed with B missing a reply and gateway logged `dispatch-from-config: skipped reply operation admission ... reason=active-run`; the new dispatch regression failed with `Slack routed thread was blocked by the active reply operation`. Before the Slack-only scoping update, the non-Slack compatibility test failed with `expected false to be true`, showing the generic guard would have changed non-Slack same-session steering.

## Root Cause

- Root cause: Slack DMs intentionally share the stable direct-message session key, but active-run decisions originally compared only session identity. A second top-level DM from the same user could therefore steer into, or later be blocked by dispatch admission for, the active run for the first Slack message thread. A later generic route-thread guard fixed Slack but also changed other IM providers that may use same-session/different-thread steering, so both guards are now constrained to Slack direct-message routed-thread turns.
- Missing detection / guardrail: Existing Slack prepare tests covered the stable DM session contract, but auto-reply routing tests did not cover same-session, different-thread active runs, Slack `TransportThreadId` DM thread replies, dispatch admission blocking, non-Slack compatibility, or every operation creation path.
- Contributing context: The fix belongs at the active-run routing and dispatch-admission boundaries, not in Slack session-key generation, because Slack DM session continuity is a shipped contract.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level that should have caught this: Unit test plus Slack prepare-path contract test.
- Target test or file: `src/auto-reply/reply/get-reply-run.media-only.test.ts`, `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts`, `src/auto-reply/reply/followup-runner.test.ts`, and `extensions/slack/src/monitor/message-handler/prepare.thread-session-key.test.ts`.
- Scenario the test should lock in: Same stable Slack DM session, different routed Slack thread id, active prior run, second Slack turn must not steer into or be dispatch-blocked by the first run; Slack DM thread replies using `TransportThreadId` get the same guard; non-Slack same-session direct-message turns remain steerable/admission-blocked as before; every created active reply operation should carry the routed thread id when one exists.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: It exercises the auto-reply and dispatch decisions that caused the overlap while separately preserving the Slack session-key contract, non-Slack compatibility, and the dispatch/follow-up operation creation paths ClawSweeper flagged.

target.title: fix(gateway): dedupe probe warnings by gateway identity
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `openclaw gateway probe` warned about multiple reachable gateways when SSH tunnel and configured remote probes reached the same gateway through different transports.
- Solution: classify reachable probe warnings by gateway self identity plus logical gateway port instead of raw reachable target count.
- What changed: gateway self presence now parses legacy `Gateway: host (ip)` text, and status warnings suppress duplicate same-identity transports while preserving distinct/ambiguous warnings.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): unknown identity probes still warn conservatively, and same-host probes on different gateway ports still warn.

## Motivation

- Avoid misleading remote-gateway diagnostics for a valid setup where both SSH tunnel and direct LAN URL point to one gateway.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #69135
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior addressed: `gateway probe` no longer emits `multiple_gateways` for two reachable transports that identify the same gateway and same logical gateway port.
- Real environment tested: WSL Ubuntu-24.04 source checkout using a Linux network namespace as the remote host, with a temporary sshd, real `/usr/bin/ssh` local port forward, direct remote WebSocket URL, and paired `operator.read` device auth.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/commands/gateway-presence.test.ts src/commands/gateway-status/output.test.ts --reporter=dot`; then `node --import tsx src/entry.ts gateway probe --ssh root@<remote-ip>:<ssh-port> --ssh-identity <temp-key> --timeout 20000 --json` against a namespace-hosted gateway with `gateway.remote.url=ws://<remote-ip>:<gateway-port>`.
- Evidence after fix: focused tests passed with 2 files and 10 tests. The live CLI proof returned `ok: true`, `degraded: false`, `warnings: []`, `primaryTargetId: "sshTunnel"`, and two reachable targets: `sshTunnel` at `ws://127.0.0.1:<gateway-port>` and `configRemote` at `ws://<remote-ip>:<gateway-port>`, both reporting the same gateway self identity and `operator.read` scope.
- Observed result after fix: same gateway identity plus same gateway port produces no `multiple_gateways` warning; distinct or ambiguous reachable probes still produce the warning.
- What was not tested: an external physical remote host; the live proof used an isolated Linux network namespace to model the remote host while still exercising `/usr/bin/ssh` and the real CLI probe path.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: `buildGatewayStatusWarnings` treated `reachable.length > 1` as multiple gateways without comparing the gateway self identity returned by each probe.
- Missing detection / guardrail: output tests did not cover multiple reachable probe targets that represent the same gateway through different transports.
- Contributing context (if known): SSH tunnel probing prepends a local tunnel URL while keeping the configured remote URL, so two reachable targets can be one logical gateway.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/commands/gateway-status/output.test.ts`, `src/commands/gateway-presence.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: same-identity reachable transports suppress `multiple_gateways`; distinct, unknown, or different-port gateways still warn.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug is in warning construction after probe results are already collected.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): none.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

`openclaw gateway probe` no longer shows the multiple-gateways warning when SSH tunnel and configured remote probes identify the same gateway.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[ssh tunnel target reachable] + [configured remote reachable] -> [reachable count > 1] -> multiple_gateways

After:
[ssh tunnel target reachable] + [configured remote reachable] -> [same self identity + gateway port] -> no warning
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: WSL Ubuntu-24.04
- Runtime/container: Node 22 repo checkout
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Gateway status/probe output
- Relevant config (redacted): `gateway.mode=remote`, `gateway.remote.url=ws://<remote-ip>:<gateway-port>`, `gateway.remote.sshTarget=root@<remote-ip>:<ssh-port>`, paired device auth with `operator.read`; remote gateway bound on LAN in a Linux netns.

### Steps

1. Run focused unit tests for SSH tunnel/configured remote warning behavior.
2. Start a temporary SSH daemon and OpenClaw gateway inside an isolated Linux network namespace.
3. Configure the probe home with both a direct remote WebSocket URL and an SSH target to that namespace.
4. Run `openclaw gateway probe --ssh ... --json` and verify both transports reach the same gateway identity with no `multiple_gateways` warning.

### Expected

- Same identity and same logical port: no `multiple_gateways`.
- Distinct or ambiguous gateway identities: keep `multiple_gateways`.

### Actual

- Matches expected after this patch.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: same identity suppression; same-host different-port warning; distinct identity warning; unknown identity warning; legacy self-presence parsing; live CLI probe with real SSH tunnel plus configured direct remote URL to the same namespace-hosted gateway.
- Edge cases checked: SSH tunnel identity uses remote gateway port instead of local tunnel port; direct remote and tunnel targets both returned the same `self` host/IP/version/platform; `warnings` was empty.
- What you did **not** verify: an external physical remote host; the namespace proof covers the same network shape in-process on WSL.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: suppressing the warning too broadly could hide a real multi-gateway setup.
  - Mitigation: the warning remains for unknown identities, distinct host/IP identities, and same-host targets on different gateway ports.

target.title: fix(config): do not suppress recovery retry after failed backup restore
target.body:
## Summary

Fix dedup-suppression bug in `maybeRecoverSuspiciousConfigRead` / `maybeRecoverSuspiciousConfigReadSync` (exported plugin SDK helpers). When a backup restore fails (e.g. EACCES), the helper unconditionally records the dedup signature, permanently blocking future recovery attempts on subsequent launches. The fix gates signature recording on `restoredFromBackup === true`.

## Scope

`maybeRecoverSuspiciousConfigRead` is exported in the plugin SDK (`packages/plugin-sdk/dist/src/config/io.observe-recovery.d.ts`) but is not currently called from the core runtime config path. Core config reads finalize through `observeConfigSnapshot` in `io.ts`, which does not call this helper. The fix ensures the SDK contract is correct for any current or future caller (plugins, `openclaw doctor`, extensions).

## Fix

In both the async and sync variants, gate `lastObservedSuspiciousSignature` recording on `restoredFromBackup === true`:

```diff
- // Always record (even on failure)
- lastObservedSuspiciousSignature = suspiciousHash;
+ // Only record on successful restore so failures don't block retries
+ if (restoredFromBackup) {
+   lastObservedSuspiciousSignature = suspiciousHash;
+ }
```

## Verification

Two-phase integration tests call the real `maybeRecoverSuspiciousConfigRead` and `maybeRecoverSuspiciousConfigReadSync` functions with real filesystem operations (temp dirs, real file writes, real `copyFile`/`copyFileSync`). The tests inject an EACCES failure into `copyFile` on the first call, then call the function again with working deps:

Phase 1 (failed restore): `maybeRecoverSuspiciousConfigRead` is called with a `deps.fs.promises.copyFile` that rejects with EACCES. The audit log records `restoredFromBackup: false` and no health-state suppression entry is written.

Phase 2 (retry succeeds): `maybeRecoverSuspiciousConfigRead` is called again with working deps. Because no suppression entry was written, the function retries the restore successfully. The audit log records `restoredFromBackup: true` and the config file is restored to the backup content.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** Config auto-restore from backup now retries on next launch after a failed `copyFile` restore. Previously, a failed restore (EACCES) wrote the "already observed" health entry, permanently suppressing future recovery attempts for that signature.

**Real environment tested:** macOS 15.5, Node 22.19, openclaw dev checkout at `3656e84c4c`

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```
$ OPENCLAW_TEST_FAST=1 pnpm test src/config/io.observe-recovery.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose
```

**Evidence after fix:** All 20 tests pass, including two new two-phase integration tests that call the actual `maybeRecoverSuspiciousConfigRead` / `maybeRecoverSuspiciousConfigReadSync` functions with real filesystem operations:

```
 ✓  retries recovery on next launch after a failed copyFile restore (4ms)
 ✓  sync recovery retries on next launch after a failed copyFileSync restore (3ms)
```

These tests exercise the full flow:
1. Seed a backup + clobbered config on real disk
2. Call `maybeRecoverSuspiciousConfigRead` with a `copyFile` that throws EACCES
3. Assert audit log shows `restoredFromBackup: false`
4. Call `maybeRecoverSuspiciousConfigRead` again with working deps
5. Assert the config is restored (`gateway.mode === "local"`) and audit log shows `restoredFromBackup: true`

Source confirmation that both guard sites are present:

```
$ grep -n 'if (restoredFromBackup)' src/config/io.observe-recovery.ts
683:  if (restoredFromBackup) {
795:  if (restoredFromBackup) {
```

Full test suite output:

```
 ✓  auto-restores suspicious update-channel-only roots from backup (12ms)
 ✓  auto-restores when metadata disappears from an otherwise valid config (4ms)
 ✓  auto-restores when gateway mode disappears from the last-good shape (3ms)
 ✓  hardens async backup restores to owner-only config permissions (2ms)
 ✓  auto-restores after a large size drop against last-good config (3ms)
 ✓  does not restore noncritical config edits (1ms)
 ✓  records copyFile failure instead of falsely claiming restore succeeded (2ms)
 ✓  sync recovery records copyFileSync failure instead of falsely claiming restore succeeded (2ms)
 ✓  retries recovery on next launch after a failed copyFile restore (4ms)
 ✓  sync recovery retries on next launch after a failed copyFileSync restore (3ms)
 ✓  deduplicates recovery for the same suspicious signature (3ms)
 ✓  deduplicates sync recovery for the same suspicious signature (2ms)
 ✓  promotes a clean config snapshot to last-known-good backup (2ms)
 ✓  recovers from last-known-good when available (2ms)
 ✓  recovers from last-known-good when backup is missing (2ms)
 ✓  does not report spurious restore when health write fails (2ms)
 ✓  sync recovery does not report spurious restore when health write fails (1ms)
 ✓  persists bounded clobbered config snapshot to disk on recovery (2ms)
 ✓  caps the number of clobbered snapshots to prevent disk growth (6ms)
 ✓  preserves the signature hash across promote+recover roundtrip (2ms)

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  20 passed (20)
```

**Observed result after fix:** The two-phase retry tests confirm that after a failed `copyFile` restore, `maybeRecoverSuspiciousConfigRead` does NOT write the suppression signature. On the next call, the function retries the restore and succeeds. The config file content is verified to contain the backup data (`gateway.mode === "local"`), not the clobbered data.

**What was not tested:** No live gateway restart cycle with injected filesystem faults was tested. The two-phase behavior was verified through integration tests that call the real exported function with real filesystem operations and injected `copyFile` EACCES failures.

target.title: fix(security): escape field names in transcript regex extraction
target.body:
## Summary

`extractJsonStringFieldPrefix` and `extractJsonNullableStringFieldPrefix` in `session-utils.fs.ts` interpolate the `field` parameter directly into `new RegExp(...)` without escaping. All current callers pass hardcoded field names (`"id"`, `"parentId"`, `"type"`, `"role"`), but the function signature accepts any `string`. A future caller passing a field containing regex metacharacters (e.g. `"foo.bar"`) would match unintended patterns (`.` matches any character instead of a literal dot).

This wraps the interpolation with `escapeRegExp()` from `src/shared/regexp.ts` so metacharacters are treated literally.

## Verification

219 session-utils tests pass (gateway-core, gateway-server, gateway-client). The new `oversized line metadata extraction preserves id and parentId` test exercises the escapeRegExp-guarded regex path.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** Regex injection via unescaped field name interpolation in oversized transcript metadata extraction (`session-utils.fs.ts:276,293`). Metacharacter field names cause extraction failure or wrong-value extraction.

**Real environment tested:** macOS arm64, Node 26.0.0, openclaw checkout at patched head.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

Standalone runtime script that replicates the vulnerable and fixed extraction functions, then runs each against JSON prefixes containing field names with regex metacharacters:

```
node --import tsx /tmp/proof-85934.mjs
```

**Evidence after fix:**

```
=== PR #85934: field name regex escape proof ===

Field with parentheses: count(items)
  Input: {"count(items)":"5","other":"x"}
  Before fix: EXTRACTION FAILED
  After fix:  5

Field with brackets: tags[0]
  Input: {"tags[0]":"first","tags0x":"other"}
  Before fix: EXTRACTION FAILED
  After fix:  first

Field with plus: rate+tax
  Input: {"rate+tax":"15","ratetax":"10"}
  Before fix: 10
  After fix:  15

Field with $: $ref
  Input: {"$ref":"#/definitions/model","ref":"fallback"}
  Before fix: EXTRACTION FAILED
  After fix:  #/definitions/model

Safe field name (normal transcript): id
  Input: {"type":"message","id":"msg-abc123","parentId":"root"}
  Before fix: msg-abc123
  After fix:  msg-abc123
  Both work correctly for safe field names.

escapeRegExp neutralizes metacharacters while safe names are unaffected.
```

Without escapeRegExp, parentheses, brackets, and dollar signs cause extraction to fail entirely, and the plus sign causes extraction of the wrong value (`10` from `"ratetax"` instead of `15` from `"rate+tax"`). With escapeRegExp, all metacharacter field names are treated literally and extract the correct value.

**Observed result after fix:** The escapeRegExp wrapper fixes four classes of metacharacter failures (parentheses, brackets, plus, dollar sign) while leaving safe field names unaffected. The plus-sign case is the most dangerous: it silently returns the wrong value instead of failing.

**What was not tested:** End-to-end production transcript pipeline with adversarial field names (all current callers use hardcoded safe strings). The fix is defense-in-depth.

target.title: feat(compaction): add identifier survival validation after summarization
target.body:
Closes #79588

## Problem

The compaction quality guard validates summary length, language, and content structure, but does not check whether opaque identifiers (UUIDs, commit hashes, API keys, session IDs) from the pre-compaction transcript survive into the post-compaction summary. When identifiers are lost during summarization, the agent loses the ability to reference specific resources, commits, or sessions mentioned earlier in the conversation.

## What changed

### `compaction-safeguard-quality.ts` (new functions)

- **`extractMessageTextForIdentifiers(message)`**: Extracts text for identifier detection from any message type, including tool call arguments (`toolCall`, `toolUse`, `functionCall` blocks) and tool result content. Skips `toolResult.details` (stripped for security).
- **`extractIdentifiersFromMessages(messages)`**: Runs the existing `extractOpaqueIdentifiers` regex pipeline over an entire message array.
- **`computeLostIdentifiers(sourceIdentifiers, summary)`**: Diffs source identifiers against a summary string. Uses case-insensitive matching for pure-hex identifiers.

### `compaction-safeguard.ts` (validation pass)

After `summarizeInStages` produces a summary:
1. Extracts identifiers from all original messages using `extractIdentifiersFromMessages`
2. Computes lost identifiers via `computeLostIdentifiers`
3. Under strict policy, if >2 identifiers are lost, triggers a re-summarization retry (within the existing `resolveQualityGuardMaxRetries` budget) with lost identifiers injected as preservation hints
4. Logs when identifiers are lost even below the retry threshold

### Tests (19 new tests)

Unit tests for extraction, deduplication, case-insensitive hex matching, policy variants (strict/off/custom), and integration tests for retry triggers.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** Compaction safeguard no longer re-appends raw identifiers after the final summary cap. The cap is a security and size boundary; bypassing it with uncapped identifier blocks violated the contract.

**Real environment tested:** macOS 15.5, Node 22.19, openclaw dev checkout at `80f1d49076`

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

Source verification that the cap boundary is respected on current HEAD:

```
$ grep -n 'postCapLost' src/agents/pi-hooks/compaction-safeguard.ts
1314:        const postCapLost = computeLostIdentifiers(transcriptIdentifiers, summary);
1315:        if (postCapLost.length > 0) {
1317:            `Compaction safeguard: final capping removed ${postCapLost.length} identifier(s) from summary.`,
```

**Evidence after fix:** Console output from `node` verification confirms no cap-violating re-append logic exists in the post-cap path:

```
$ node -e "
  const src = require('fs').readFileSync('src/agents/pi-hooks/compaction-safeguard.ts','utf8');
  const capSection = src.slice(src.indexOf('postCapLost'), src.indexOf('postCapLost') + 500);
  console.log('contains identifierBlock re-append:', capSection.includes('identifierBlock'));
  console.log('contains summary concatenation:', /summary\s*\+=/.test(capSection));
  console.log('contains Exact identifiers heading:', capSection.includes('Exact identifiers'));
  console.log('logs warning only:', capSection.includes('final capping removed'));
  console.log('cap boundary respected:', !capSection.includes('identifierBlock') && !(/summary\s*\+=/.test(capSection)));
"
contains identifierBlock re-append: false
contains summary concatenation: false
contains Exact identifiers heading: false
logs warning only: true
cap boundary respected: true
```

**Observed result after fix:** Actual output from `node` reading the quality-loop shows identifier preservation instructions remain active for the LLM summarizer (pre-cap), while the post-cap check is diagnostic-only:

```
$ node -e "
  const src = require('fs').readFileSync('src/agents/pi-hooks/compaction-safeguard.ts','utf8');
  console.log('IDENTIFIER_PRESERVATION_INSTRUCTIONS present:', src.includes('IDENTIFIER_PRESERVATION_INSTRUCTIONS'));
  console.log('computeLostIdentifiers in quality loop:', (src.match(/computeLostIdentifiers/g)||[]).length, 'calls');
  console.log('extractOpaqueIdentifiers present:', src.includes('extractOpaqueIdentifiers'));
  const postCapBlock = src.slice(src.lastIndexOf('computeLostIdentifiers'), src.lastIndexOf('computeLostIdentifiers') + 300);
  console.log('post-cap block is warn-only:', postCapBlock.includes('log.warn') && !postCapBlock.includes('summary +='));
"
IDENTIFIER_PRESERVATION_INSTRUCTIONS present: true
computeLostIdentifiers in quality loop: 3 calls
extractOpaqueIdentifiers present: true
post-cap block is warn-only: true
```

**What was not tested:** No live agent session with a large transcript containing many opaque identifiers was tested. The cap-exceeding scenario requires a summary that barely fits the cap with identifiers at the tail.
